proposition_text: The Environment Agency has published its strategy EA2030, outlining clear goals to support healthy air, land, and water, while promoting nature's recovery, sustainable growth, and climate resilience.
The Water (Special Measures) Act 2025 introduces a statutory requirement for water companies to produce and publish annual Pollution Incident Reduction Plans (PIRPs).
Pollution Incident Reduction Plans (PIRPs) set out short-term, tactical actions to reduce the frequency and severity of pollution incidents.
Drainage and Wastewater Management Plans (DWMPs) are statutory plans that outline long-term strategies to improve and maintain a resilient drainage and wastewater system.
In 2024, over 800,000 tonnes of biosolids were produced by water companies in England, with 93% reused to support soil and agriculture.
All storm overflows in England now have Event Duration Monitors installed, with spill performance reports published annually.
The Water (Special Measures) Act 2025 requires water companies to install Event Duration Monitors on all emergency overflows at pumping stations: 50% by 2030 and full coverage by 2035.
The Water (Special Measures) Act 2025 will speed up the Environment Agency's enforcement response by imposing faster and more predictable civil penalties for less serious levels of offending.
The Water (Special Measures) Act 2025 provides the Environment Agency with the power to recover enforcement costs from water companies.
In 2024, serious pollution incidents from the water and sewerage sector increased by 60%, from 47 in 2023 to 75 in 2024.

In 2024, 1 of the 9 water and sewerage companies was rated as poor performing, 7 were rated as requiring improvement, and 1 was rated as industry leading under the Environmental Performance Assessment.
In 2024, farming activities caused 74 serious pollution incidents, increasing from 53 in 2023, representing 12% of all serious pollution incidents in 2024.
Dairy farming caused 48 serious pollution incidents in 2024, with 92% of dairy incidents involving silage or slurry.
In 2024, 51% of non-permitted farm inspections found non-compliance with environmental regulations, with insufficient slurry storage capacity identified at 33% of inspected farms.
The Slurry Infrastructure Grant aims to provide storage capacity of at least 6 months for beef and dairy cattle and 8 months for pigs.
The Slurry Infrastructure Grant provides partial funding to improve slurry storage, covering 50% of the costs of replacing, expanding, covering or building additional stores.

A farming business in Lancashire was prosecuted for two counts of illegal discharge and one count of failing to comply with slurry storage requirements, following investigation of two separate slurry incidents that polluted a local watercourse.
In 2024, the waste sector had the highest number of serious pollution incidents compared to other sectors regulated by the Environment Agency, accounting for 23% of all serious incidents.
In 2024, 146 serious pollution incidents were recorded from permitted and exempt waste management activities at 50 sites, a 57% increase compared with 93 incidents in 2023.
In 2024, 150 (91%) of persistent poor performer permits were in the waste sector; persistent poor performers are those with permits in bands D, E or F for 2 or more consecutive years.

In 2024, the waste sector demonstrated compliance with 91% of its permits (performance bands A and B), compared to 95% compliance for other industrial sectors regulated through the Environmental Permitting Regulations.
In 2024, prosecution cases against the waste sector led to total fines of around £320,000 and 37 custodial sentences.

The Environment Agency decided on 6 March 2025 to exercise its statutory powers under Regulation 57 of the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016 to arrange steps to remove risks of serious pollution from Walleys Quarry Landfill Site.
Phase 1 rollout of digital waste tracking will become mandatory for all waste receiving sites from October 2026.
Waste crime is estimated to cost the country £1 billion per annum.
In 2024 to 2025, the Environment Agency stopped 743 illegal waste sites, of which 143 were high risk.
In 2024, the Environment Agency prevented the illegal export of 79,713 tonnes of waste.
In 2024, the UK Emissions Trading Scheme for installations and for aviation achieved compliance rates of 98%.

The Energy Savings Opportunities Scheme (ESOS) requires large companies and company groups to undertake an accredited energy assessment to identify opportunities to reduce energy usage and greenhouse gas emissions.

Under the Clean Heat Market Mechanism, manufacturers supplying gas and oil boilers in the UK market must meet targets for the installation of heat pumps in existing properties, set in proportion to their sales of fossil fuel boilers.

Operators using AI methods, including machine learning and large language models, must meet environmental compliance standards and provide clear documentation, validation, and auditable systems suited to the regulatory context.

During 2024, operators and permit holders in scope of climate change adaptation requirements were required to produce an initial risk assessment to understand the specific risks of climate change to and from their business.
In 2024, the Environment Agency brought 168 prosecution cases, 98 of which resulted in fines totalling £3.8 million.
In 2024, the Environment Agency issued 295 enforcement notices, almost double that in 2023.
In 2024, the Environment Agency accepted 52 enforcement undertakings totalling £4 million in donations to environmental projects or improvements.
In 2024, the Environment Agency imposed 1 civil sanction (variable monetary penalty) under the Water Resources Act for £19,778.
In 2024, 97% of permits regulated under the Environmental Permitting Regulations remained in the top three compliance bands (A to C), consistent with levels since 2013.
In 2024, there were 622 serious pollution incidents, 38% of which involved permitted activities.

The Environment Agency has begun publishing Compliance Assessment Reports (CARs) online for sectors regulated under the Environmental Permitting Regulations, replacing the previous request-based system.
Storm overflows account for 7% of waterbodies failing to reach Good Ecological Status.
Industrial sectors regulated under the Environmental Permitting Regulations were responsible for 15 serious pollution incidents in 2024, down from 33 in 2020, representing 2% of the national total.
In 2024, 25% of wastewater treatment works inspected had breaches of permit conditions.
The Environment Agency's water transformation programme has created over 500 roles focused on water industry regulation since it was set up in 2023.
The Environment Agency more than tripled the number of wastewater treatment works inspections to over 4,650 in 2024 to 2025.
In 2024 to 2025, the Environment Agency carried out 4,767 targeted farm inspections at 4,348 non-permitted farms.
In 2024, there were 978 inspections of farms permitted under the Environmental Permitting Regulations, of which 98% of permits were in the highest compliance bands (A and B).
Between 2022 and 2024 there were over 1,300 enforcement actions against farming businesses, with 15 being prosecutions.

An offender convicted of waste crime offences in 2021 was ordered in February 2025 to pay an additional £278,493 under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 after previously undeclared assets were discovered.
In 2024, the Environment Agency's water transformation work was funded through changes to charges applied to water quality discharge permits, increasing income by £51 million per year.
Industrial decarbonisation schemes administered by the Environment Agency cover over 40% of the UK's carbon emissions and have achieved high compliance rates.
In 2024, the UK ETS for installations operators delivered an 11.1 million tonne reduction of carbon dioxide compared to 2023.

The Environment Agency's Definition of Waste Service has provided 10 formal opinions this year, enabling 362,700 tonnes of material to be placed back on the market as non-waste, delivering annual savings to industry of over £19 million.
In 2024, 92% of water abstraction data returns were submitted digitally via the Manage your Water Abstraction Service.
The Environment Agency has established standard rules permits for low-risk research and development activities at permitted industrial installations, published in March 2025.
Each Mid Tier multi-year option and capital item has an associated code, name, and payment rate.

To receive support and approval from a Catchment Sensitive Farming Officer (CSFO), the options and capital items applied for must address the water or air pollutants identified as affecting that catchment or area.
An applicant must clearly show how water pollutants are currently reaching a watercourse or underground aquifer in order to receive CSFO support and approval.
An applicant must identify where ammonia emissions are coming from in order to receive CSFO support and approval for air quality options.
An applicant must show how the proposed work will prevent losses of water pollutants or ammonia in order to receive CSFO support and approval.
CSFO support will be targeted at farms where the largest improvements in water and air quality are likely to be achieved.
The impact of a water pollutant or ammonia on a sensitive protected site will be considered as part of CSFO support targeting.
An applicant must show that they have included a suitable range of options and capital items to prevent the pollutant reaching a watercourse or the atmosphere.
An applicant must agree with their CSFO how work is phased where it needs to be carried out over a number of years.
An applicant must contact their local CSFO at least 6 weeks before intending to submit an application, otherwise there is no guarantee the request will be considered.
An application receives a higher score if it meets local water and air quality objectives set out in the local Statement of Priorities.
Certain options and capital items require approval from a local CSFO before an applicant may apply for them.
If an applicant applies for CSFO-approval-required options or capital items without having that approval, those items will be removed from the application.
Land must be in a Countryside Stewardship High Priority Area for Water or Air Quality in order to be eligible for options and capital items requiring CSFO approval.
An applicant whose land is in a Countryside Stewardship High Priority Area for Water or Air and who wants CSFO support or approval must contact their local CSFO as early as possible.
An applicant must contact their local CSFO at least 6 weeks before intending to submit their application to request help or a visit.
When contacting a CSFO, an applicant must provide their name, address and postcode.
When contacting a CSFO, an applicant must provide their County Parish Holding (CPH) and Single Business Identifier (SBI) numbers.
When contacting a CSFO, an applicant must provide details of the capital items or options they are considering which will benefit water or air quality.
When contacting a CSFO, an applicant must provide information on water or air quality priorities on their farm.
When contacting a CSFO, an applicant must provide basic farm details such as size and enterprise type (for example, dairy, arable, poultry).

When contacting a CSFO, an applicant must provide information on how the pollutant is currently reaching a watercourse, or in the case of air where ammonia emissions are coming from, and how they intend to prevent this.
Any waste sent for biological treatment must be capable of being treated by the process.
Dilution is not considered a suitable waste treatment for biological treatment processes.
Biological processes can degrade complex synthetic organic substances.
The products resulting from biological degradation processes may pose a significant threat to human health and the environment.
Operators must fully characterise and assess all bespoke wastes before introducing them into a biological treatment process.
If an operator accepts a bespoke waste type, the site permit must include the relevant List of Waste (LoW) code and description as set out in technical guidance WM3.
Where an operator accepts similar bespoke waste from a different producer, that waste requires its own pre-acceptance assessment.
An operator may need to apply for a permit variation when accepting similar bespoke waste from a different producer, to ensure pre-acceptance processes are part of their operational techniques.
Operators may need to test the resulting outputs from biological treatment to ensure the material has been fully treated.
Biological treatment facilities may need to comply with The Animal By-Products (Enforcement) (England) Regulations 2013 to accept and treat animal by-products.
Animal by-products compliance for biological treatment facilities is regulated by the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA).
Biological organic treatment facilities can be authorised to accept category 3 animal by-products.
AD plants where the only feedstock is grown energy crops or by-products from food waste production do not currently need an environmental permit or exemption for the digestion process.
Operators of AD plants using only energy crops or food waste by-products will need a permit for any combustion unit (engine, boiler or generator).
AD plants taking mixed feedstocks (energy crops, slurry, manure and waste) require an environmental permit for the digestion process.
AD plants taking mixed feedstocks (energy crops, slurry, manure and waste) require an environmental permit for any associated combustion unit or specified generator.
Materials produced incidentally to a process, such as clean-down or wash waters, leachates and liquors from feedstock storage, are waste.
Transfer and disposal of waste must comply with the duty of care code of practice under section 34(7) of the Environmental Protection Act 1990.
Measures with an equivalent annual net direct cost to business of below (plus or minus) £5 million are certified as being below de minimis under the Business Impact Target.
The Environment Agency created new Regulatory Position Statement RPS 233: Treating microbiological laboratory waste on the site where it is produced.
The Environment Agency created new Regulatory Position Statement RPS 234: Accepting, classifying and disposing of household packaging waste.
The Environment Agency created new Regulatory Position Statement RPS 235: Treating and using water that contains concrete and silt at construction sites.
The Environment Agency created new Regulatory Position Statement RPS 239: Temporary storage of recyclable waste at collection points for charities.
The Environment Agency revised Regulatory Position Statement RPS 55: Dewatering and depositing silts from sustainable drainage systems on land.
The Environment Agency withdrew Regulatory Position Statement RPS 56: Treatment by laboratory autoclave of containment level 1 to 3 microbiological waste on the premises it is produced.
The Environment Agency extended Regulatory Position Statement RPS 211: Excavated waste from utilities installation and repair.
The Environment Agency extended Regulatory Position Statement RPS 207: Classifying waste wood from mixed waste wood sources.
The Environment Agency created new Low Risk Waste Position LWRP 74: Burning dunnage packing material.
The Environment Agency created new Low Risk Waste Position LWRP 76: Recycling waste spectacles.
The Environment Agency published new standard rules SR2020 No 1 covering storage of Publicly Available Specification (PAS 107) certified clean cut tyre shred and chip at a port.
The Environment Agency published new guidance on healthcare waste: appropriate measures for permitted facilities.

The Environment Agency created a new regulatory position statement on slurry stores on permitted pig and poultry farms with less than 1% dry matter to ensure compliance with Directive 2010/75/EU on industrial emissions.

The Environment Agency extended Regulatory Position Statement RPS 228: Storing and treating waste plastic produced from processing waste electrical and electronic equipment, to comply with Article 7 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 on persistent organic pollutants.

The Environment Agency published new guidance on chemical waste: appropriate measures for permitted facilities to ensure compliance with Directives 2008/98/EC on waste and 2010/75/EU on industrial emissions.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for regulating major industry and waste.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for treatment of contaminated land.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for water quality and resources.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for fisheries.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for inland river, estuary and harbour navigations.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for conservation and ecology.
Within England, the Environment Agency is responsible for managing the risk of flooding from main rivers, reservoirs, estuaries and the sea.

11,883 permitting determination decisions were made during the BIT reporting period 13 December 2019 to 16 December 2020, of which 1,550 were permit variations to secure required environmental outcomes from the water company periodic review.
1,517 permitting determination decisions were registered and 1,087 exemptions for flood risk activities were registered under the environmental permitting regime during the BIT reporting period.
163 determination decisions were made across the entire radioactive substances regulation environmental permitting regime during the BIT reporting period.
462 permits were issued for the movement of live fish to or from fisheries during the BIT reporting period.
519 authorisations were issued for commercial fishing in the 2020 calendar year; authorisations are granted on a seasonal basis and are compiled over each calendar year.
4 appeals were received against permitting decisions during the BIT reporting period 13 December 2019 to 16 December 2020.
4 independent internal reviews were carried out under the provisions of the Regulators' Code during the BIT reporting period.
8,896 compliance assessment report (CAR) forms were issued to permitted waste operations and facilities carrying out industrial processes during the BIT reporting period.
4,017 CAR forms and letters were issued for permitted water discharge activities during the BIT reporting period.
856 CAR forms were issued to water abstraction and impoundment licence holders during January to September of the BIT reporting period.
792 CAR forms were issued across the radioactive substances regulation environmental permitting regime during the BIT reporting period.
304,672 exempt activities were registered and 1,739 were de-registered under the Environmental Permitting Regulations during the BIT reporting period.
501 commercial boats were registered during the BIT reporting period.
498 consents were issued for international waste shipments during the BIT reporting period, 2 of which were for radioactive wastes.
12,469 registrations and 165 compliance assessment actions were carried out under the Producer Responsibility laws during the BIT reporting period.
295 permitting decisions were made under Climate Change Trading and Regulatory Services during the BIT reporting period.
441 compliance checks were completed under Climate Change Trading and Regulatory Services during the BIT reporting period.
846 entrants were registered and 114 were de-registered under Climate Change Trading and Regulatory Services during the BIT reporting period.
7 appeals were received under Climate Change Trading and Regulatory Services during the BIT reporting period.
68 civil penalty notices were issued under Climate Change Trading and Regulatory Services during the BIT reporting period.
48 prosecution cases were completed by the Environment Agency during the BIT reporting period.
8 formal cautions were accepted by the Environment Agency during the BIT reporting period.
203 enforcement notices were served by the Environment Agency during the BIT reporting period.
993 warning letters were sent by the Environment Agency during the BIT reporting period.
Civil sanctions were used on 74 occasions by the Environment Agency during the BIT reporting period; these are sanctions issued under the Regulatory Enforcement and Sanctions Act 2008.
There were 5 national external consultations conducted by the Environment Agency during the BIT reporting period 13 December 2019 to 16 December 2020.
The spreading of manure provides a route by which bacteria can pass from livestock to the natural environment, food crops, other animals and people.
Bacteria can develop resistance to antibiotics, leading to decreased treatment options for the infections they cause.
Attempts should be made to reduce the spread of antibiotic-resistant bacteria wherever possible.
The Animal by-products Regulation (EC1069/2009) defines manure as 'any excrement and/or urine of farmed animals other than farmed fish, with or without litter'.
Article 9 of the Animal by-products Regulation (EC1069/2009) classifies manure as a category 2 Animal by-product (ABP).
The Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) is responsible for enforcing the Animal by-products (ABP) regulation.
Where APHA identifies a risk to human or animal health associated with manure or slurry, specific control measures may be put in place, usually in relation to notifiable disease.
If APHA does not consider there to be a risk of spreading any serious, transmissible disease, manure can be applied to land without processing under Authorisation B1.
It is recommended that manure is stacked for a period of 8 weeks to reduce the number of bacteria that could present a danger to public or animal health.
Once a manure stack is formed, no further manure should be added to the stack for the entire 8-week period.
The manure stack should be sited so as to prevent livestock gaining access.
If possible, the manure stack should be sited on hard standing and covered once formed, to prevent access by wildlife (including birds).
Following the 8-week stacking period, manure can be spread on land.
Manure can be sent for composting at a designated facility.
There is no requirement to use an ABP-approved composting plant for the disposal of manure (unlike other materials such as food waste).
If the farm has adequate storage capacity, it is recommended that slurry is held for a period of 3 months prior to spreading on land.
During the 3-month slurry holding period, no further slurry is to be added.
Both slurry and manure can be sent to an anaerobic digestion plant.
There is no requirement to use an ABP-approved anaerobic digestion site for the disposal of manure (unlike other materials such as food waste).
It is best practice to spread slurry or manure on arable land rather than pasture which will be grazed or cropped for animal feed, in order to reduce transmission of bacteria to other animals.

If manure or slurry is applied to grazing land or land cropped for feed production, it is recommended that grazing or cropping does not take place for a period of at least 8 weeks, to reduce the risk of spreading antibiotic-resistant bacteria to livestock.
Injecting slurry rather than top spreading reduces the spread of resistance by reducing runoff and minimising wildlife exposure.

It is advisable to take appropriate hygiene and bio-security precautions when spreading or handling manure, for example wearing outer clothing and waterproof footwear which can be cleaned and disinfected, wearing gloves, and hand washing.
Manure must not be stored or treated anywhere near food crops which are eaten without cooking, such as salads.
Authorisation D10 allows the transport of manure between two points located on the same farm, or between farms and users of manure.
Manure being transported under Authorisation D10 should be transported in covered leak-proof containers in conditions which prevent unacceptable risks to animal and public health.
Additional requirements apply for farms or fields located in nitrogen vulnerable zones.
Stacked manure and slurry that has been batch-stored for a minimum period of 6 months prior to spreading may be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops any time before drilling or planting.

Composted solid manure or lime-treated slurry that has been stored to allow maturation for at least 3 months prior to spreading may be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops any time before drilling or planting.
Solid manure and slurry that is untreated or does not meet the specified treatment conditions should not be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops within 12 months of harvest.

Manure or slurry applied to land used to grow ready to eat vegetables should be incorporated into the soil as soon as is practicable, to reduce the potential for direct crop contamination and to reduce odour and ammonia emissions.

Digestate from slurry or manure processed in an anaerobic digestion plant that complies with BSI PAS 110 requirements may be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops any time before drilling and planting.
Natural England will continue to deliver the £30 million Nutrient Mitigation Scheme in line with the Environment Secretary's direction of 28 July 2022.
Nutrient mitigation credits for 4,200 new homes have already been provided by the Nutrient Mitigation Scheme in the Tees and Cleveland Coast catchment.
The Nutrient Mitigation Scheme will continue to operate on the principle of complementing locally and privately led mitigation schemes, rather than crowding out these schemes.
Natural England will continue to provide advice and guidance to other scheme operators, helping to secure and grow the potential pipeline of credits.

The £110 million Local Nutrient Mitigation Fund will enable local authorities to boost the supply of mitigation by investing in innovative mitigation schemes and enabling additional mitigation credits.

Funding from the Local Nutrient Mitigation Fund will be recycled locally until nutrient mitigation is no longer needed, at which point it will be invested in measures to help restore the relevant Habitats Sites.
DLUHC has confirmed the first tranche of capital funding with up to £57 million of funding to the 8 successful bids under the Local Nutrient Mitigation Fund.
DLUHC has confirmed a second round of Nutrient Support Funding with another £100,000 to a lead authority for substantive catchments (those over 10,000 hectares in size).
Round 2 of the Local Nutrient Mitigation Fund is open to Expressions of Interest until 26 April 2024.

The Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 created a new duty for water companies in designated catchments to ensure wastewater treatment works serving a population equivalent over 2,000 meet specified nutrient removal standards.

Competent authorities (including local planning authorities) considering planning proposals for development draining via a sewer to a wastewater treatment works subject to the upgrade duty are required to consider that the nutrient pollution standard will be met by the upgrade date for the purposes of Habitats Regulations Assessments.

On 25 January 2024, the Secretary of State for the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs designated 16 catchments in which water companies are required to upgrade wastewater treatment works before 1 April 2030.
A limited exemption process for wastewater treatment works exemptions will be completed by 1 April 2024.

Wastewater treatment works exemptions confirmed by 1 April 2024 may affect the levels of nutrient mitigation that development must secure when connecting to specific wastewater treatment works in some catchments.
Planning decisions must continue to be taken based on material planning considerations.
The government will invest £200 million in grants for improved slurry storage infrastructure and equipment over the agricultural transition period.
The government will commit £25 million to a new nutrient management theme within the Farming Innovation Programme to help farmers manage plant and soil nutrients.
The government will introduce payment premiums into environmental land management schemes in 2024, including for options that provide benefits for water quality.

The government will continue to conduct at least 4,000 risk-based inspections on farms each year to ensure slurry and other pollutants are handled in a way that minimises pollution of the water environment.
The government will consult on mandating Sustainable Drainage Solutions for relevant new developments, subject to a threshold, to reduce urban run-off and pressure on storm overflows and flood risk.

Defra launched an additional round of the Natural Environment Investment Readiness Fund, which opened for applications on 11 December, to help farmers address barriers to accessing private investment for nature's recovery including through nutrient mitigation projects.
A river basin is the area of land from which all surface water run-off flows through a sequence of streams, rivers and possibly lakes into the sea at a single river mouth or estuary.

A river basin district includes the area of land and sea made up of one or more neighbouring river basins together with their associated groundwaters and coastal waters, covering one nautical mile from the coast.
The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) (England and Wales) Regulations 2017 require the preparation and publication of river basin management plans.

The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) (England and Wales) Regulations 2017 require the setting of environmental objectives for groundwater and surface waters, including estuaries and coastal waters.
The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) (England and Wales) Regulations 2017 require the devising and implementing of programmes of measures to meet environmental objectives.
Under the WFD Regulations, a river basin management plan must be developed for each river basin district.
Under the WFD Regulations, a river basin management plan must be reviewed and updated every 6 years.
River basin management plans were first published in December 2009, updated in February 2016, and updated again in December 2022.
The river basin management plans will be reviewed and updated again by December 2027.
All public bodies are under a duty to 'have regard to' the river basin management plans.
The Environment Agency and Natural England produce diffuse water pollution plans for some habitats sites.
'Habitats sites' refers to 'European sites' and 'European marine sites' as defined in regulation 8 of the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017 (as amended).
New activities or developments must comply with constraints identified in river basin management plans.

The Marine Management Organisation issues marine licences for activities in and around the estuaries and sea of English waters, including construction, dredging, removal, and deposit of substances and objects at sea.
The Environment Act 2021 requires the government to always have an Environmental Improvement Plan in place.
The government must review its Environmental Improvement Plan every 5 years.
The WFD Regulations require river basin planning data to be presented at river basin district level.
The 25 Year Environment Plan was designated as the first statutory Environmental Improvement Plan under the Environment Act 2021.
Only invited applicants can apply for round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant is only available for slurry stores located in England.

Slurry is defined as liquid or semi-liquid matter composed of excreta produced by livestock while in a yard or building (including that held in wood chip corrals); or a mixture wholly or mainly consisting of livestock excreta, livestock bedding, rainwater and washings from a building or yard used by livestock, of a consistency that allows it to be pumped or discharged by gravity at any stage in the handling process.
A grant-funded slurry store must be fitted with an impermeable cover, unless the applicant is installing a slurry bag or treating slurry through acidification.

The Slurry Infrastructure grant uses standard costs to determine the maximum grant amount; the grant will pay the standard cost per item or 50% of the total invoice costs, whichever is the lower amount.
The Rural Payments Agency will prioritise projects which have the greatest environmental benefit.
If the Slurry Infrastructure Grant round 1 is oversubscribed, RPA will prioritise projects in areas that need urgent action to reduce nutrient pollution from agriculture or restore natural habitats.
A farmer may apply for a Slurry Infrastructure grant if their farming system already produces slurry and they farm pigs, beef, or dairy.
Both land-owning and tenant farmers can apply for the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
The slurry store funded by the grant must be built on land the applicant owns or farms as a tenant.
A tenant farmer applying for the Slurry Infrastructure grant is responsible for obtaining any necessary permissions from their landlord.

A tenant farmer applying for the Slurry Infrastructure grant must confirm they will have a tenancy agreement in place for the duration of the grant funding agreement (5 years after the date of final payment).
A farmer cannot get Slurry Infrastructure grant funding if they already have 6 months' serviceable slurry storage.
A farmer cannot get Slurry Infrastructure grant funding if they do not currently produce slurry.
A farmer cannot get Slurry Infrastructure grant funding if they intend to use the store for anything other than storing slurry.

Poultry farmers, sheep farmers, arable-only farmers, horticultural growers, farming businesses that only provide contractor services, public organisations (including Crown bodies and non-departmental public bodies), and local authorities are not eligible for the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
All grant-funded items must meet the specifications set out for the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
New or expanded grant-funded stores must be fitted with an impermeable cover unless the applicant is installing a slurry bag or using acidification.
Acidification is the use of acid treatment or other technologies (such as plasma treatment) to lower and maintain the pH value of slurry to below 6 at the point of storage.
To be exempt from covering a store under the Slurry Infrastructure grant, the applicant must be able to acidify all their slurry before they apply.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant cannot be used to pay for slurry acidification systems.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant can only be used for covers for new or expanded stores; it cannot be used for a cover only.
If an applicant intends to purchase second-hand slurry store accessories, they must obtain confirmation from the supplier that the item has a 5-year life and has not received previous grant funding.
A grant applicant must not commit to any eligible costs (including paying deposits) before the start date on their grant funding agreement.
A grant applicant must not place an order for grant-funded items before the start date on their grant funding agreement.
A grant applicant must not start construction work before the start date on their grant funding agreement.
Costs incurred before the start date on the grant funding agreement are at the applicant's own risk and may make the whole project ineligible if it is considered to have been started.
Grant-funded projects must maintain 6 months' storage capacity for all animals on the holding for the duration of the grant funding agreement, irrespective of future livestock numbers.
The grant will only cover up to 6 months' worth of storage based on existing livestock numbers, even if the applicant plans to expand capacity beyond 6 months.
Existing livestock numbers means the typical number of animals kept on the farm over the last year, counted from the point the full application is submitted.
If a grant recipient increases their herd size, they must make appropriate upgrades to their storage and slurry management to maintain 6 months' storage capacity.
The grant may be used to add additional storage only if other stores that will remain part of the system meet slurry storage regulations, were built to relevant standards and remain fit for purpose.
If a grant recipient replaces a store that is not fit for purpose, they must stop using the old store for slurry or other organic material.
Farmers planning slurry storage must follow the Water Resources (Control of Pollution) (Silage, Slurry and Agricultural Fuel Oil) (England) Regulations 2010.
Farmers planning slurry storage must follow the Nitrate Pollution Prevention (Amendment) Regulations 2016.
Farmers planning slurry storage must follow the Reduction and Prevention of Agricultural Diffuse Pollution (England) Regulations 2018.
Farmers planning slurry storage must follow the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016, which include protections for groundwater.
The minimum grant that can be applied for under the Slurry Infrastructure grant is £25,000.
The maximum Slurry Infrastructure grant is £250,000 for each applicant business.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant is paid in arrears.
A grant recipient can make a maximum of 3 claims over the duration of the Slurry Infrastructure grant project.
A grant recipient must have sufficient funds to pay for all items in full before claiming a Slurry Infrastructure grant payment.
Other public money (for example grant funding from local authorities) must not be used towards the Slurry Infrastructure grant project costs.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant must not be used to carry out capital works which are required under other agreements.
The final Slurry Infrastructure grant payment will be based on the standard cost contribution for items needed or 50% of the actual invoice costs at claim stage, whichever is the lower amount.
A grant recipient must ensure no items in their application are replacements covered by an insurance claim.

Monies from the Slurry Infrastructure grant must not be used to cross-subsidise any related, linked, parent, subsidiary, partnership, or joint venture businesses or operations based in Northern Ireland.
Funds from the Slurry Infrastructure grant must be used solely for the purposes of England business operations.
Only invited applicants can apply for round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant is competitive and applicants will not automatically receive a grant.
The online checker for the Slurry Infrastructure grant closed at 11:59pm on 31 January 2023 and no new applications can be accepted.
RPA will email applicants with a decision on whether or not their project has been shortlisted for Round 2.
RPA will either invite applicants to submit a full application or inform them that they have not been successful.
Storage plans for the Slurry Infrastructure grant must meet storing slurry rules, farming rules for water (FRfW) and regulations protecting groundwater.
The funded project must allow at least 6 months' storage capacity.
Applicants invited to make a full application must provide the size, location and type of new stores.
Applicants must provide the size, location and condition of existing stores if they form part of the 6 months' storage capacity, including photographs.
Applicants must provide a copy of their storage capacity calculation using the slurry wizard.
Applicants must provide rainfall records if not using slurry wizard rainfall data.
Applicants must provide scaled drawings and design specifications of the proposed structures.
Applicants must provide location maps of the proposed structures including any springs, wells or boreholes within their farm holding.
Applicants must provide maps of farmyard areas, including drainage.
Applicants must provide information on soil suitability if constructing or expanding an earth bank lagoon.
Applicants must provide written confirmation from the original manufacturer or a suitably qualified engineer that an existing store is suitable for expansion and covering, if using that option.
Applicants must provide details of the secondary containment they will deploy if they intend to use a large volume supported slurry bag.

Only invited applicants can apply for round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.

Slurry is defined by SSAFO as liquid or semi-liquid matter composed of excreta produced by livestock while in a yard or building (including that held in wood chip corrals), or a mixture wholly or mainly consisting of livestock excreta, livestock bedding, rainwater and washings from a building or yard used by livestock, of a consistency that allows it to be pumped or discharged by gravity at any stage in the handling process.
Dirty water and lightly fouled water are both forms of slurry.
A farmer must make sure their storage calculation includes all forms of slurry as required by SSAFO, including runoff from stacked farmyard manures not in temporary field heaps.
A farmer must include runoff from bedding materials (for example woodchips, straw-bedded corrals, stand-off pads) in their slurry storage calculation.
A farmer must include the liquid element of separated slurry runoff from stacked separated slurry solids in their slurry storage calculation.
A farmer must include separated slurry from weeping wall stores or strainer boxes in their slurry storage calculation.
A farmer must include rainwater that falls directly into a store or flows into the store system in their slurry storage calculation.
A farmer must include wash water from fouled concrete areas (including parlour washing) in their slurry storage calculation.
A farmer must include rainwater runoff from fouled yards or concrete areas in their slurry storage calculation.
Any effluent (such as parlour washings or yard run-off) previously consented to discharge directly to groundwater or surface waters must now be directed to the slurry store and treated as slurry.
Wash water and rainwater runoff may be kept in a separate store from the main slurry store but must still be stored and handled as slurry under SSAFO, NVZ and farming rules for water regulations.

Water from farm buildings and yards where livestock do not have routine access, used for other farm activities (for example sprayer washing areas), does not need to be stored as slurry if it does not drain into the slurry system.
Rainwater that falls onto uncontaminated parts of the yard or buildings does not need to be stored as slurry if it does not drain into the slurry system.
Runoff from temporary field heaps does not need to be stored as slurry if it does not drain into the slurry system.
A farmer's slurry storage calculation must include rainwater from uncontaminated parts of the yard if the system routes that water to the slurry store.

In a Slurry Infrastructure grant application, the applicant must show in their slurry wizard report that they will have enough storage to contain slurry for at least 6 months once the project is complete.
The 6-month storage requirement exceeds the 4-month minimum specified in SSAFO and the 5-month minimum required in NVZs for cattle.
The 6-month storage requirement matches the minimum required in NVZs for pigs.
Slurry stores must not be within 10 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters, including those that are seasonally dry.

For the purposes of a Slurry Infrastructure grant application, the 10-metre distance from inland freshwaters or coastal waters must be measured from the outer edge of the structure (for example, the bottom edge of the dry embankment of a slurry lagoon).
Slurry must not be stored within 50 metres of any spring, well or borehole.
The 50-metre distance from any spring, well or borehole must be measured from the maximum slurry extent (for example, the top of the wet embankment of a slurry lagoon).

A farmer must consider risk factors relating to preventing pollution from runoff, leaching, leaks, store failure or flooding — including slope, ground cover, soil type and presence of land and yard drains — when locating a slurry store.
Groundwater protection is included in the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016.
The Environment Agency normally opposes the establishment of new slurry storage within inner source protection zones (SPZ1).

If a grant applicant has no alternative to siting slurry storage within an inner SPZ1, the Environment Agency will work with the applicant to identify a location on their landholding that is of lowest risk to drinking water supplies or agree additional mitigating measures.
If a farmer replaces a slurry store that is not fit for purpose, they must stop using the old store for slurry or other organic material.
A farmer may only keep existing stores as part of their slurry system if the stores meet current regulations and build standards.
A farmer may only keep existing stores as part of their slurry system if the stores are fit for purpose.
A store is no longer fit for purpose if it has reached the end of its design life and is susceptible to leaks or failure.
To expand an existing store, the applicant must provide written confirmation that the whole store is structurally suitable for expanding and covering.
To expand an existing store, the applicant must provide written confirmation that the whole store will meet the specifications, including current regulations and build standards, when complete.
For stores built to a proprietary design (for example circular steel tanks), confirmation for expansion must come from the original manufacturer.

For stores built to a non-proprietary design (for example lagoons and rectangular stores), confirmation for expansion must come from a suitably qualified civil or structural engineer who is a member of a chartered institute.
The farming rules for water (FRfW) require farmers to plan the application of nutrients to cultivated land to meet soil and crop nutrient needs and not exceed these levels.
There are limits on the total amount of nitrogen a farmer can apply, and on when and where they can apply it in nitrate vulnerable zones (NVZs).
All successful applicants to the Slurry Infrastructure grant must have and use a nutrient management plan based on up-to-date soil sampling.
Only invited applicants can apply for round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant will be run over multiple years to give as many farmers as possible the chance to upgrade their slurry systems.
If the Slurry Infrastructure grant scheme is oversubscribed, RPA will limit the number of farmers invited to submit a full application.
RPA will give priority to Slurry Infrastructure grant projects that will most benefit the environment, based on their location.
Agriculture accounts for up to 60% of nitrate pollution and 25% of phosphate pollution in waterways.
Agriculture accounts for 87% of UK ammonia emissions.
The Slurry Infrastructure Grant - Round 1 priority areas include Sites of Special Scientific Interest (SSSIs).
The Slurry Infrastructure Grant - Round 1 priority areas include Special Areas of Conservation (SACs).
The Slurry Infrastructure Grant - Round 1 priority areas include Special Protection Areas (SPAs).
The Slurry Infrastructure Grant - Round 1 priority areas include wetlands designated to be of international importance under the Ramsar Convention (Ramsar sites).
RPA will prioritise areas within 2km of protected sites that contain a known feature sensitive to atmospheric ammonia.
RPA will prioritise areas within 2km of protected sites that currently exceed their atmospheric ammonia critical level.
An atmospheric ammonia critical level is the threshold at which adverse effects are known to occur for a sensitive feature.
RPA will prioritise the 2014 Countryside Stewardship water quality priority areas (CS14), updated to include a small number of new sites with diffuse water pollution issues.
Applicants must provide an Ordnance Survey grid reference for the proposed slurry store in the online checker.
When the online checker closes, RPA will assess how many Slurry Infrastructure grant projects can be awarded a grant.
If demand is high, RPA will plot each project against the priority area using the Ordnance Survey grid references to produce a shortlist.

If there are still too many projects after initial shortlisting, RPA will select the highest priorities for water and focus on the air quality sites at most risk of damage by ammonia or nitrogen deposition.
RPA will tell all applicants whether their Slurry Infrastructure grant project has been shortlisted or not.
RPA will adapt the approach to targeting in future rounds of the Slurry Infrastructure grant to support as many farmers as possible.
Only invited applicants can apply for round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
Round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant is to improve or expand slurry storage capacity to 6 months based on existing livestock numbers.
The Slurry Infrastructure grant value is between £25,000 and £250,000.
A farmer may apply for the Slurry Infrastructure grant only if they farm pigs, beef or dairy cattle.
A farmer may apply for the Slurry Infrastructure grant only if their farming system already produces slurry.
Both land owning and tenant farmers can apply for the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
The slurry store funded under the Slurry Infrastructure grant must be built on land the applicant owns or farms as a tenant.

Applicants must complete their application and email it with any supporting documents to ftf@rpa.gov.uk.

Any farm is eligible for inspection by the Environment Agency, as all farms have the potential to cause pollution.
A farm holding Environment Agency permits or licences may receive more frequent inspections as a requirement of the permit or licence.
A farm may be visited by inspectors if a pollution incident is thought to be on or caused by that farm, following an inspection or report.
Inspectors may combine a farm visit with an intensive pig and poultry permit inspection.
Inspectors may carry out a visual inspection of farm premises and selected field activities.
Inspectors may inspect a farmer's records or management plans.
A farm operator will normally be told which records will be inspected before the visit.
Inspectors may check that a farm operator is following any Environment Agency permit or licence conditions.
Most farm inspections will cover pollution prevention and control.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (NVZ) regulations where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with Environmental Permitting Regulations (EPR) groundwater permits where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with abstraction licensing requirements where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with sludge and industrial waste land spreading deployment requirements where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with the Sludge (Use in Agriculture) Regulations where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with EPR intensive pig and poultry permit conditions where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with agricultural waste exemption requirements where relevant to the reason for inspection.
Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with hazardous waste requirements where relevant to the reason for inspection.

Inspectors may check whether a farm operator is complying with the Water Resources (Control of Pollution) (Silage, Slurry and Agricultural Fuel Oil) (England) Regulations (SSAFO) where relevant to the reason for inspection.
A farm inspection takes on average 2.5 hours.
A farm operator will receive up to 14 days' notice of a planned inspection.
Advance notice of an inspection is not possible when the Environment Agency is responding to a pollution incident.
Inspectors will try to avoid visiting farms during busy times in the farm calendar.
Certain permits and licences have set inspection frequencies.
A farm holding a groundwater permit will be visited by inspectors every 6 years.
The Environment Agency will send a farm operator a letter after an inspection visit, stating any issues found and what corrective action is required.

Where inspectors find non-compliances, the Environment Agency may issue verbal advice, depending on the severity of the non-compliance, any pollution caused, and any actions taken to become compliant.

Where inspectors find non-compliances, the Environment Agency may issue a warning letter, depending on the severity of the non-compliance, any pollution caused, and any actions taken to become compliant.
Where inspectors find non-compliances, the Environment Agency may issue a caution, depending on the severity of the non-compliance, any pollution caused, and any actions taken to become compliant.
Where inspectors find non-compliances, the Environment Agency may serve a notice, depending on the severity of the non-compliance, any pollution caused, and any actions taken to become compliant.
The Environment Agency will prosecute farm operators in some cases of non-compliance.

Catchment Sensitive Farming (CSF) works with farmers and partners across England to produce food in a way that protects water, air, and soil.
CSF advisers (CSFAs) are locally based with an understanding of the challenges farmers face.

CSF provides locally informed, confidential, on-farm advice to help farmers make decisions on soil management, nutrient, slurry, and manure management, ammonia emission reduction, farm infrastructure and machinery set-up, pesticide handling, water resources and natural flood management, local environmental priorities, land management, and agricultural transition including grants.
A farmer may request CSF advice by sending a completed Catchment Sensitive Farming Advice Request Form to their local mailbox.

CSF will reply based on the environmental priorities in the farmer's area and will either arrange a one-to-one consultation with the local CSFA within an agreed timeframe, or link to other sources of advice and information.
CSF focuses its support on farms that can make the greatest improvement to water and air quality.
CSF works with partners including water companies, local authorities, trade bodies, environmental organisations, and river trusts.
Organisations interested in working with CSF may contact CSF by emailing csf.partnerships@naturalengland.org.uk.
Applicants must read the Capital Grants 2026 guidance to understand the rules and how to apply for item AQ2.
Applicants applying for item AQ2 as part of a Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) application must read the CSHT applicant's guide to understand the rules and how to apply.
The payment rate for item AQ2 low ammonia emission flooring is £72 per square metre (m²).
Reducing the mixing of urine and faeces on livestock housing floors and rapidly removing urine limits the chemical reactions that release ammonia into the air.
Low emission floors are typically used with suitable slurry scrapers to reduce the surface area of slurry exposed to the air, thereby reducing ammonia emissions.
Item AQ2 may only be used with support from a Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser.
Item AQ2 may be used on holdings targeted for the reduction of ammonia emissions from agriculture, as confirmed by a Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser.
Item AQ2 may be used on livestock buildings with solid or slatted floors in slurry-based livestock housing.
Item AQ2 must not be used where solid flooring is going to be fitted on top of slatted flooring with underfloor slurry storage.
Item AQ2 must not be used where urine and faeces would not separate effectively on the floor due to the dry nature of manure produced.
The grant holder must install low ammonia emission flooring in livestock housing (such as grooved rubber flooring) designed to separate and rapidly drain urine.
The grant holder must fit the flooring to the full passageway width.
Where slatted floors are covered, the grant holder must cover them to match the area, gaps and slats of the floor.
The grant holder must use flooring suitable for the livestock being housed.
The grant holder must ensure the flooring can be retrofitted to solid concrete or slatted floors.
The grant holder must install flooring specifically designed to reduce ammonia emissions by at least 25% compared to conventional flooring.

The ammonia emission factor (EF) or percentage ammonia emissions reduction must be verified by a published peer-reviewed research paper, official European design standard, or a testing system report (for example an RAV official test reference number).
The grant holder must use flooring suitable for the dimensions and slope of the floor area.
The grant holder must use flooring suitable for the location where it will be installed.
The grant holder must use flooring suitable for the type and number of livestock housed.
The grant holder must use flooring suitable for the amount of slurry produced.
In cattle housing, the grant holder must install flooring that separates urine from faeces effectively and quickly directs urine away to storage.
In cattle housing, the grant holder must scrape the floor with a scraper that is effective for use with the flooring installed.
In cattle housing, the grant holder must scrape the floor at a frequency in line with the product specification (at least 4 times a day) to remove faeces to storage.
The grant holder must keep written support from their Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser and provide it with their application.

The grant holder must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the site during the different stages of construction, or contracts, invoices or other documents confirming the technical specification for the completed works.
The grant holder must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the completed works.

The grant holder must keep and provide on request the technical specification of the product showing evidence of ammonia emission factor (EF), or percentage reduction in ammonia emissions, and related verification documentation.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request receipted invoices or bank statements where a receipted invoice is unavailable.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request photographs of the site before works start.
Applicants applying for item AQ2 as part of a Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) application must read the record keeping and site visit requirements in the CSHT agreement holder's guide.

Grant recipients must complete the claim form in their GFA and email it to FETFClaims@rpa.gov.uk by midday 10 January 2025.
Grant recipients must submit a separate claim and evidence for each grant they are applying for.
After a claim is submitted, the grant recipient's business may be chosen for a site visit before the RPA makes the grant payment or up to 5 years after payment.
Grant recipients must submit copies of invoices for items bought as part of their claim evidence.
Grant recipients must submit copies of business bank, building society or business credit card statements showing that items have been paid in full.
Grant recipients must submit photographic evidence of each item as part of their claim.
The RPA may reject all or part of a claim if the correct evidence is not submitted.
The RPA cannot accept evidence sent using secure data transfer sites on the internet, such as OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive or Apple iCloud.
Invoices must show the cost of each item.
Invoices must include the manufacturer's make and model.
Invoices must quote the relevant FETF 2024 item code from the item and specification list.
Invoices must be addressed to the same individual or business as the GFA.
Invoices must show the supplier's name, address, VAT number and date.
Invoices must be marked by the supplier to show a refundable deposit was used to buy the item, if applicable.
If an invoice includes items not in the GFA, the grant recipient must clearly show which items they are claiming for.
Grant recipients must use a business account or credit card (demonstrably paid by the business account) to pay for grant-funded items.
Grant recipients must not use cash or a personal account to pay for grant-funded items.
Grant recipients must not use lease or hire purchase to pay for grant-funded items.
Grant recipients must not use part exchange to pay for grant-funded items.
Grant recipients must submit copies of bank, building society or credit card statements so the RPA can check invoices have been paid in full by the business named in the GFA.
Statements submitted as evidence must show the bank or building society's name and logo.
Statements submitted as evidence must show the account holder name in full.
Statements submitted as evidence must show the account number.
Statements submitted as evidence must show the transaction date.
Statements submitted as evidence must show the transaction type, including payee reference or cheque number.
Statements submitted as evidence must show the transaction amount.
If a grant recipient pays for an item through a Buying Group, they must provide evidence that they have paid the Buying Group.
If a grant recipient pays for an item through a Buying Group, they must provide evidence that the Buying Group has paid the supplier.
Photographs submitted as claim evidence must be submitted as JPEG files or images in Word documents.
Photographs must show the item on site without packaging.
Photographs must be clear, in focus, in colour and with no other objects in front of the item.
Photographs must be bigger than 600 x 400 pixels.
Photographs must not identify individuals, including employees.
The RPA may request more photographs as evidence to show the item meets the specification and is on site.
A grant recipient may withdraw their claim at any time by emailing FETFClaims@rpa.gov.uk with their reasons and FETF reference number.
Payments are made to the bank or building society account attached to the business's main Single Business Identifier (SBI) number in the Rural Payments service.
The RPA may recover some or all of a grant payment if the grant recipient does not keep grant-funded items at the location stated in the GFA for 5 years from the date the claim was paid.
Grant recipients must keep items operational and in good repair for 5 years from the date the claim was paid.
Grant recipients must use items for the same purpose as set out in their application for 5 years from the date the claim was paid.
If there is a change in circumstances which could affect rights and obligations under the GFA, the grant recipient must tell the RPA as soon as possible.
Grant recipients must keep paperwork associated with their GFA for 7 years from the date their claim was paid.
The Farming Equipment and Technology Fund (FETF) 2024 is closed for applications.
FETF 2024 includes 3 grants to help farmers buy equipment or technology to improve productivity.
FETF 2024 includes 3 grants to help farmers buy equipment or technology to manage slurry.
FETF 2024 includes 3 grants to help farmers buy equipment or technology to improve animal health and welfare.
FETF 2024 provides grant funding of up to £50,000 towards productivity and slurry items.
FETF 2024 provides grant funding of up to £25,000 towards animal health and welfare items.
The Environment Agency corporate scorecard is reported every 3 months to executive directors and the board within the Environment Agency.
The Environment Agency Corporate Scorecard is shared with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) and is published publicly.
The Environment Agency uses a red-amber-green system to indicate performance: green means at or above target, amber means falling slightly short, and red means improvements are required.
The Environment Agency corporate scorecard measures contribute towards the wider Defra single departmental plan.

The Environment Agency worked with partners to enhance the water environment by 227km in quarter three 2018/19, bringing the cumulative total for the financial year to 1,426km against a target of 2,000km.
It is unlikely that the water environment enhancement target of 2,000km will be met for 2018/19, partly due to prolonged dry weather and the setting of a very ambitious target.
For the last 12 months to quarter three 2018/19, a total of 514 serious pollution incidents were recorded, which may rise by up to 10% when all investigations are completed.
The Environment Agency exceeded its ambitious ceiling target of 400 serious pollution incidents in quarter two 2018/19, attributable in part to prolonged dry weather and extreme summer temperatures.
Compared to quarter three 2017/18, serious pollution incidents for the last 12 months increased by 30%, from 394 to 514 incidents.
The Farming Rules for Water represent a change from an advisory to a regulatory approach from April 2019, which will help address inappropriate spreading.
During quarter three 2018/19 the number of active high risk illegal waste sites decreased from 260 to 233; this is the lowest total since the beginning of 2012.
The Environment Agency referred over 40 illegal waste sites to HMRC as part of enforcing the expanded scope of landfill tax.
41 separate flood risk projects better protected 9,111 homes in quarter three of 2018/19, bringing the cumulative total since April 2015 to 155,385 homes.
The Environment Agency is forecasting to exceed the target of 185,000 homes better protected from flooding by the end of quarter four 2018/19.
The flood risk management programme remains on track to better protect 300,000 homes by the end of March 2021.

Where flood and coastal risk management assets are below the required condition, this identifies that work is required but does not mean the assets have structurally failed or that performance in a flood is compromised.

If the performance of a flood or coastal risk management asset is reduced, the Environment Agency will take action to ensure that flood risk is effectively managed until the asset is fully repaired or replaced.
The National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) requires an assessment of flood risk to be undertaken before determining a planning application for sites in a flood plain.
The Environment Agency estimates it has 6,698 trained and capable incident staff ready to respond to incidents.
The Environment Agency has invested 69% of its full year budget in the first three quarters of 2018/19, ahead of the proportion invested to the same point last year (68%).
Carbon dioxide emissions from the Environment Agency in quarter three 2018/19 are 9% under target at 19,346 tonnes, and 836 tonnes lower than quarter three 2017/18.
The Environment Agency's 2020 carbon dioxide emissions target is 32,501 tonnes.
The Environment Agency's 2020 target for the proportion of BAME employees is 14%; the proportion was 4.0% in quarter three 2018/19.
The Environment Agency's 2020 target for the percentage of female Executive Managers is 50%; the actual percentage in quarter three 2018/19 was 34.1%.
The Lost Time Incident Frequency Rate (LTIFR) is a universally accepted lagging indicator of health and safety performance.
The Environment Agency adopts a conservative definition of lost time injury, capturing any injuries resulting in a day or more of lost time.
The Environment Agency's LTIFR ceiling target is 0.11 injuries per 100,000 hours worked; the rate in quarter three 2018/19 was 0.13.
Applicants must read the Capital Grants 2026 guidance to understand the rules and how to apply for item AQ1.
Applicants applying for item AQ1 as part of a Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) application must read the CSHT applicant's guide to understand the rules and how to apply.
Item AQ1 pays £2,760 per passageway or channel.
Removing slurry more frequently from livestock housing reduces the levels of ammonia released into the air, improving air quality and the natural environment.
An applicant with support from a Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser may use item AQ1 on holdings targeted for the reduction of ammonia emissions from agriculture.
An applicant with support from a Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser may use item AQ1 in livestock housing with impermeable or slatted floors.
Item AQ1 may be used within passageways where slurry is deposited.
Item AQ1 may be used in slurry channels within livestock housing systems.
Item AQ1 must not be used in livestock systems where the type of manure produced (such as higher dry matter) means the slurry scraper would not be effective.
Item AQ1 must not be used in livestock systems where there is an uneven or cracked floor meaning a slurry scraper would not be effective.
Item AQ1 must not be used where the automatic scraper is unlikely to reduce ammonia emissions.
Item AQ1 must not be used where the applicant needs to repair or replace part of an existing automatic scraper system.

The grant holder must install an automatic slurry scraping system with a floor scraper that runs automatically using a hydraulic ram and ratchet drive system, or a rope or chain and winch system with an electric motor.
The grant holder must make sure the scraper works effectively so slurry can be cleared from an area and stored appropriately.
The grant holder must use a scraper that fits the width of the passageway or channel.
The grant holder must use a scraper that is suitable for the type of livestock housed.
The grant holder must use a scraper that is suitable for the volume of slurry produced.
The grant holder must keep written support from their Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser and provide it with their application.
The grant holder must keep and provide with their claim contracts, invoices or other documents confirming the technical specification for the equipment and installation.
The grant holder must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the equipment in place and working.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request receipted invoices, or bank statements where a receipted invoice is unavailable.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request photographs of the site before work starts.
Applicants applying for item AQ1 as part of a CSHT application must read the record keeping and site visit requirements in the CSHT agreement holder's guide.
Item AQ1 supports the cost of an automatic scraper for one passageway or channel.
The grant holder may apply for additional scrapers for more passageways or channels if needed.
The funding under item AQ1 is for a new or complete replacement of an existing automatic scraper system.
The more frequently slurry is removed from passageways and channels, the greater the reduction in ammonia emissions.
Spreading milk on land following an animal disease outbreak is a waste operation that legally requires an environmental permit.
The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement if the activity meets the description set out in RPS 40.
The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement if the operator complies with the conditions set out in RPS 40.
The activity must not cause (or be likely to cause) pollution of the environment or harm to human health.
The activity must not cause a risk to water, air, soil, plants or animals.
The activity must not cause a nuisance through noise or odours.
The activity must not adversely affect the countryside or places of special interest.
RPS 40 applies to spreading milk on land following an animal disease outbreak identified by the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA).
The operator must only spread waste milk that has been identified by APHA for disposal.
The operator must only spread waste milk that has been disinfected as part of disease control measures required by APHA.
The operator must only spread waste milk that has been stored for less than 24 hours.
The operator must only spread waste milk that has been diluted with at least an equal amount of water or slurry (or both).
The operator must only spread waste milk at an application rate of no more than 20 cubic metres per hectare.
The operator must only spread waste milk on land where there is a low risk of runoff.
The operator must only spread waste milk once in any 4-week period.
The operator must only spread waste milk once they have informed their local Environment Agency office.
The operator must not spread milk to land if APHA has prohibited disposal by this means.
The operator must not spread waste milk if the land is waterlogged, frozen or covered with snow, or has been frozen for 12 hours or more in the 24 hours before spreading starts.
The operator must not use land for grazing or feeding crops for pigs for at least 2 months after spreading waste milk.
The operator must not use the land for any animals except pigs for at least 3 weeks after spreading waste milk.
The operator must keep records for 2 years from the date of the last use of RPS 40 to show compliance with the RPS.
The operator must make their compliance records available to the Environment Agency on request.
Land with a low risk of runoff has an average slope of less than 3 degrees.
Land with a low risk of runoff does not have land drains other than sealed impermeable pipes.
Land with a low risk of runoff has not been pipe drained, mole drained or sub-soiled in the last 12 months.
Land with a low risk of runoff does not have a shallow soil less than 30cm above fissured rock.
Land with a low risk of runoff has soil of a sufficient depth and suitable type above groundwater to prevent pollution.
Land with a low risk of runoff is not within a designated groundwater source protection zone 1.
Land with a low risk of runoff is at least 50 metres from surface water or a conduit leading to surface water.
Land with a low risk of runoff is at least 50 metres from springs, wells and boreholes where groundwater is used for human consumption.
Land with a low risk of runoff does not have compacted soil or a capped soil surface; milk may only be spread where the soil is permeable and has a good structure.
Land with a low risk of runoff does not have cracked soil above a land drainage system or groundwater.
The Environment Agency intends to review RPS 40 by 1 December 2027.
The Environment Agency may withdraw or amend RPS 40 before the review date if it considers this necessary, including where the activity the RPS relates to has not changed.
RPS 40 remains in force until it is removed from GOV.UK or is otherwise identified as having been withdrawn.
If an operator under RPS 40 can no longer comply with it, they must stop the activity to which RPS 40 relates.
If an operator under RPS 40 can no longer comply with it, they must tell the Environment Agency immediately.
