Cleaning and disinfecting after an outbreak of animal disease produces more washwater with higher concentrations of disinfectant than routine cleaning.
If a notifiable disease is confirmed on premises, the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) will advise the occupier on cleaning standards and options available.
In most notifiable disease outbreaks, the occupier of the premises is responsible for cleansing and disinfection and its cost.
The occupier of premises must follow the instructions the APHA officer gives on how to carry out cleansing and disinfection.
The occupier of premises must use a disinfectant approved by Defra for the relevant disease.
When cleaning and disinfecting a free-range poultry farm, poultry sheds should be moved to an area where washwater can be contained, where possible.
The most environmentally-friendly option for washwater disposal is to discharge washwater from cleaning and disinfecting to a foul sewer.
The occupier of premises must get approval from their wastewater service supplier before discharging disinfectant washwater to a foul sewer.
When disposing of washwater as waste, the occupier must check that their waste carrier is registered to dispose of waste.
When disposing of washwater as waste, the occupier must not allow their waste carrier to dispose of the waste illegally.
An occupier must have an environmental permit to carry out landspreading of washwater, unless the Environment Agency says one is not needed.
If the occupier wants to dispose of washwater by landspreading, they must contact the Environment Agency first to determine whether a permit is required.
The occupier must not landspread disinfectant washwater until the Environment Agency has either confirmed that no permit is needed or has granted a permit.
APHA may ask the occupier to store washwater for a set time period before landspreading.
The occupier must dispose of washwater at least 10 metres from rivers, streams and field ditches.
The occupier must dispose of washwater at least 30 metres from streams with nature conservation status.
The occupier must dispose of washwater at least 50 metres from boreholes, wells and springs used for drinking water or food production purposes.
The occupier must dispose of washwater at least 250 metres from solution features such as sinkholes, swallow holes and swallets.
Permit applications for landspreading disinfectant washwater can take up to 20 working days.
The occupier must pay an application fee when applying for a permit to landspread disinfectant washwater.
The amount of the application fee for landspreading washwater will depend on the volume and materials used.
The occupier should use existing dirty water or effluent management storage systems to store disinfectant washwater before disposal, if possible.
Existing drains that serve yards and livestock buildings should connect to the effluent management system, as unconnected drains can lead to uncontrolled polluting discharges.
The occupier should avoid using a slurry store for disinfectant washwater.
If disinfectant washwater is added to a slurry store, all of the disinfectant washwater becomes waste.
If disinfectant washwater is added to a slurry store, the occupier must apply for an environmental permit to landspread the entire contents of the store.
The occupier must agree the location and construction of temporary washwater storage systems with the Environment Agency before building them.
If the occupier builds a temporary lagoon for washwater storage, they must meet the rules for storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil (SSAFO).
A temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must be 10 metres from watercourses or ditches.
A temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must be 50 metres from wells, springs or boreholes used for drinking water or food production.
A temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must have a base above the water table.
A temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must have a gap of at least 750 millimetres between the surface of the lagoon's content and the top of the lagoon wall.
A temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must have a liner, unless there is at least 1 metre of clay subsoil below the base.
Liners for a temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must be made from butyl rubber or plastic (PVC or high or low density polyethylene).
A temporary lagoon used for washwater storage must not use a low grade liner in a high risk groundwater area.
Earth bank stores should not be filled above ground level.
The occupier must not use temporary washwater storage for more than 12 months.
An occupier who allows washwater or disinfectant to cause pollution by entering groundwater through uncontrolled seepage could face enforcement action and an unlimited fine.
An occupier who allows washwater or disinfectant to cause pollution by entering surface waters such as ditches, rivers or coastal waters could face enforcement action and an unlimited fine.
The occupier must fulfil their business or commercial waste disposal responsibilities when disposing of disinfectant washwater.
Round 2 of the Improving Farm Productivity grant is closed for applications.
A farmer or horticulturalist in England may apply for the Improving Farm Productivity grant.
Contractors may apply for the Improving Farm Productivity grant if they have a registered business address in England.
A contractor is defined as a business (including a sole trader) that carries out an agricultural or horticultural activity as a service.
Contractors are not eligible to apply for solar PV grant funding.
The grant funded assets must be located in England.
The Improving Farm Productivity grant must not be used to subsidise any related business or operations in Northern Ireland.
If a grant funded asset is mobile, the applicant must store and keep it in England when not in use.
Both land owning and tenant farmers can apply for the Improving Farm Productivity grant.
The project must be on land the applicant owns or farms as a tenant.
A tenant applicant is responsible for obtaining any necessary permissions from their landlord.
A tenant on a tenancy of less than 5 years which they expect to continue may sign a grant funding agreement on their own.
A tenant applicant may ask their landlord to underwrite their grant funding agreement, meaning the landlord agrees to take over the project if the tenancy ends.

Government departments, executive agencies, non-departmental public bodies (NDPBs), and any public body where the business forms part of their obligations as a public body are not eligible for the Improving Farm Productivity grant.
Members of Producer Organisations under the Fresh Fruit and Vegetables Aid Scheme are not eligible for the grant if they have funding for the same project through their operational programme.
Forestry businesses are not eligible for the Improving Farm Productivity grant.

For applications covering robotic or automatic equipment, wavelength specific LED lighting and/or advanced ventilation control units (excluding solar PV), the minimum grant is £25,000 (50% of £50,000).

For applications covering robotic or automatic equipment, wavelength specific LED lighting and/or advanced ventilation control units (excluding solar PV), the maximum grant is £500,000 per applicant business.
For applications covering solar PV equipment only, the minimum grant is £15,000 (25% of £60,000).
For applications covering solar PV equipment only, the maximum grant is £100,000 per applicant business.
An applicant wishing to apply for both a Solar grant and a Farm Productivity grant must submit 2 separate applications.
The maximum grant across all submitted applications is £500,000 in total per applicant business.
An applicant must be able to pay the remaining project costs not covered by the grant.
An applicant may use loans, overdrafts, and certain other monies (such as Basic Payment Scheme or agri-environment scheme payments) to pay the remaining project costs.
An applicant cannot use other public money, such as grant funding from local authorities, towards the project costs.
The Improving Farm Productivity grant cannot be used to carry out capital works which the applicant is required to do as part of other agreements.
For robotic equipment to be eligible, it must perform all 4 functions—sensing, understanding, planning, and controlling—in a continuous loop without human intervention.
Other robotic or automatic technology not listed in the eligible equipment list will also be considered for the grant.
Solar PV panels funded by the grant can only be installed on farm building rooftops or irrigation reservoirs.
Upgrades to electricity supply to the holding are eligible only up to a maximum of 10% of the project's total eligible costs.
Eligible Farm Productivity equipment using an electric or renewable energy source will be prioritised over fossil fuel powered equipment.
Biofuels are considered as renewable energy under the Improving Farm Productivity grant scheme.
The grant cannot be used to fund equipment used for the packing and processing of agricultural products such as eggs, fruit and vegetables.
The grant cannot be used to fund other renewable energy generation projects such as anaerobic digester plants.
The grant cannot be used to fund solar panels on a north facing roof or one that is heavily shaded.
The grant cannot be used to fund a ground based solar array.
The grant will not cover projects that are carried out only to meet a legal requirement.
The grant will not cover any costs incurred before the project start date shown in the grant funding agreement.
The grant will not cover contingency costs.
The grant will not cover the cost of obtaining permissions, consents or statutory requirements, such as planning permission.
The grant will not cover legal costs.
The grant will not cover any items for which the applicant has already received EU or public funding, or intends to receive public funding.
The grant will not cover relocation costs if the business needs to relocate to expand.
The grant will not cover construction of new buildings or conversions of existing buildings, including flooring for robotic milking parlours.
The grant will not cover repairs and maintenance of existing buildings, equipment, and machinery.
The grant will not cover like-for-like replacement of existing items such as buildings, equipment and machinery.
The grant will not cover purchase of machinery or equipment that will not be on the asset register of the business 5 years after completion of the project.
The grant will not cover replacement of existing solar PV panels.
The grant will not cover salaries and running costs of the business.
The grant will not cover in-kind contributions, such as the cost of using the applicant's own labour, vehicle, and office space.
The grant will not cover recurring monthly or annual licence fees, subscriptions, and service charges.
The grant will not cover reclaimable VAT.
The Rural Payments Agency will score applications against the funding priorities and award grants to the highest scoring applications.
If demand is high, the RPA will prioritise projects that best meet the scheme priorities.
Methane is a more potent greenhouse gas than carbon dioxide per tonne, but shorter lived in the atmosphere.
Methane is a significant precursor to tropospheric ozone, a harmful air pollutant that affects public health, ecosystems and crops.
The Climate Change Act 2008 sets a legally binding target for the UK to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to net zero by 2050.
The Climate Change Act 2008 covers carbon dioxide emissions alongside methane, nitrous oxide (N2O) and four f-gases known collectively as super pollutants.
UK methane emissions have reduced by 62% between 1990 and 2023, one of the highest percentage reductions among OECD countries.
The Global Methane Pledge is a collective voluntary commitment of all signatories to reduce global methane emissions by 30% by 2030 on a 2020 baseline.
Agriculture and waste sectors' emissions policies are devolved to the nations of the UK (England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland).
Agriculture, waste and fuel supply account for 87% of UK territorial methane emissions.
The agriculture sector represents 48% of the UK's methane emissions, having fallen by 16% from 1990 to 2023.
The UK's cattle population produced 78% of agricultural methane emissions in 2023 (65% from digestion and 12% from manure management).
Slurry Infrastructure grant-funded stores require methane-mitigating impermeable covers.
Methane Suppressant Feed Products (MSFPs) can reduce methane emissions from 5–30% per cow when mixed into cattle feed.
The UK government intends to mandate the use of proven safe and effective Methane Suppressant Feed Products in England as soon as feasibly possible, and by 2030 at the latest.
The UK government intends to consult in late 2025 on proposals for a mandatory disease eradication programme for Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) in England.
Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) increases cows' methane emissions.
The waste sector generates 31% of the UK's methane emissions.
Waste sector methane emissions have fallen by 74% since 1990.
Landfill accounts for 81% of the waste sector's methane emissions.

The UK packaging Extended Producer Responsibility (pEPR) requires some organisations and businesses to pay a fee for the packaging they import from 2025, to support better waste collecting, managing, recycling and disposal.
The UK Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) will require the use of a refundable deposit for certain single-use drink containers from 1 October 2027.
Simpler Recycling in England requires food waste to be collected from all workplaces (except micro-firms) in England.
Simpler Recycling food waste collection will be extended to households in England by 31 March 2026.
Simpler Recycling food waste collection will be extended to micro-firms (with fewer than 10 FTE) in England by 31 March 2027.
Simpler Recycling requires food waste to be sent to recycling (anaerobic digestion) or composting only, and never to residual waste treatment including incineration or landfill.
As of 2023, upstream oil and gas made up less than 2% of all UK methane emissions, while the total fuel supply sector made up around 8%.
The fuel supply sector's methane emissions have fallen by 88% since 1990.
The North Sea Transition Authority (NSTA) requires industry to publish Emissions Reduction Action Plans.
The NSTA reports annually on progress of emission reductions from UK oil and gas production, including public benchmarking showing flaring and venting emissions by named oil and gas assets.
Flaring on the UK continental shelf has decreased by an estimated 51% between 2018 and 2024.
Since 2000, methane emissions from gas leakage in the mains network have fallen by 50%, through the Iron Mains Risk Reduction Programme (IMRRP).
Plastic gas mains pipes have 99% lower leakage rates than the iron pipes they replace.
The UK oil and gas sector achieved the Oil and Gas Climate Initiative's 2025 methane intensity target of 'well below' 0.20% at 0.18% in 2020.
The NSTA requires zero routine flaring and venting by 2030.

In January 2025, Health and Safety England published new rules requiring Gas Distribution Networks to better monitor condition and detect leaks of all iron mains, not just those 'at risk', and use Advanced Leakage Detection techniques to improve leakage sensitivity.
Government intends to consult on a future biomethane policy framework in financial year 2025–26.
The financial incentive provided by the Renewables Obligation will end for most landfill gas to electricity generators from 2027.
The Northern Ireland Government must reduce municipal waste to landfill to less than 10% by 2035.
The UK digital waste tracking service will be in place from April 2026 to help tackle illegal waste handling.
The UK's Climate Change Act 2008 framework will continue to drive action to reduce methane emissions at the pace necessary to meet carbon budgets and deliver net zero by 2050.
Spreading slurry or milk during exceptional weather or on unfavourable soil can cause soil damage and water pollution.
A farmer must contact the Environment Agency if their slurry or milk store is at risk of overflowing or leaking.
A farmer must contact the Environment Agency if they cannot avoid spreading slurry or milk on agricultural land and there is a risk of slurry runoff, run-through to land drains or leaching.
A farmer may only spread milk to land if they have a U10 waste exemption.
Exceptional circumstances are those that are not common, usual or reasonably expected.
Exceptional circumstances do not apply to circumstances that can be planned for, such as wetter than average winter rainfall.
A farmer must have a contingency plan to avoid causing pollution during exceptional circumstances.
A farmer must make sure that all staff and contractors are aware of the contingency plan.
A farmer's contingency plan should include field inspections to consider the risk of slurry or milk getting into surface water or groundwater.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should reduce the amount of slurry (including lightly fouled water) they produce.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should wash dairy parlours down with a low volume hose system of 0.6 cubic metres per cow per month or 20 litres per cow per day.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should remove excess dung with a brush or squeegee before hosing down to reduce the amount of wash water needed.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should keep animals on straw to produce solid manure rather than slurry.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should divert uncontaminated surface water away from dirty yards.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should keep or move livestock onto the smallest yard area necessary.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer should install, maintain or repair gutters and downpipes, especially on roofs that drain onto dirty yards.
A farmer must normally comply with SSAFO rules to store slurry.
The Environment Agency will waive the full SSAFO requirements if a farmer wants to store slurry for less than 12 months.
A farmer must only consider temporary slurry storage where existing facilities are inadequate.
To keep slurry in a temporary store, a farmer must check planning requirements with their local planning authority.
To keep slurry in a temporary store, a farmer must contact the Environment Agency before construction.
To keep slurry in a temporary store, a farmer must agree each individual location with the Environment Agency.
To keep slurry in a temporary store, a farmer must install tanks, liners and slurry bags to manufacturer's instructions.
The base of earth bank lagoons used for temporary slurry storage must be above the water table, with at least one metre of clay subsoil beneath the proposed base.
A farmer must use a trial pit to confirm the depth of the clay layer, and the resulting hole must be backfilled and puddled in.
A farmer must use liners where there is doubt about soil permeability for temporary slurry storage, using high grade liners in high risk areas.
A farmer must monitor temporary slurry storage to make sure there are no leaks.
A temporary slurry store must be located at least 10 metres from watercourses and land drains.
A temporary slurry store must be located at least 50 metres from groundwater sources.
A farmer must de-commission a temporary slurry store as soon as it is no longer needed.
Where shared slurry storage facilities are used, the farmer must consider any biosecurity risks.
Where shared slurry storage facilities are used, the farmer must agree management arrangements.
Where shared slurry storage facilities are used, the farmer must agree where responsibility lies.
A farmer must spread slurry or milk thinly and widely.
A farmer must spread slurry or milk at an application rate not exceeding 20 cubic metres per hectare, and must use a lower application rate if run-off could enter surface water.
A farmer must only spread slurry or milk on land with low run-off risk.
Low run-off risk land has an average slope of less than 3 degrees.
Low run-off risk land does not have land drains other than sealed impermeable pipes.
Low run-off risk land has not been pipe drained, mole drained or sub-soiled in the last 12 months.
Low run-off risk land does not have a shallow soil less than 30cm above fissured rock.
Low run-off risk land has a sufficient depth and suitable type of soil above groundwater to prevent pollution.
Low run-off risk land is not within a designated groundwater source protection zone 1.
Low run-off risk land is at least 50 metres from surface water or a conduit leading to surface water.
Low run-off risk land is at least 50 metres from springs, wells and boreholes where groundwater is used for human consumption.
Low run-off risk land does not have compacted soil or a capped soil surface; a farmer may only spread where the soil is permeable and has a good structure.
Low run-off risk land does not have cracked soil above a land drainage system or groundwater.
Mixing slurry with milk increases the risk of lethal or explosive gases such as methane, carbon dioxide, ammonia and hydrogen sulphide.
If a farmer spreads slurry and milk during exceptional circumstances without causing pollution, the Environment Agency may decide not to take enforcement action.
A farmer must contact the Environment Agency before spreading any slurry or milk to land during exceptional circumstances.
A farmer must agree with the Environment Agency that spreading is the only option available before spreading slurry or milk during exceptional circumstances.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer must only spread the amount of slurry and milk needed to reduce the risk of pollution.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer must only spread up to 20 cubic metres per hectare and on the lowest risk land available.
During exceptional circumstances, a farmer must carry out regular checks before, during and after spreading to ensure there is no pollution taking place.
A farmer is still responsible for any pollution that they cause during exceptional circumstances.
Applicants must read the Capital Grants 2026 guidance to understand the rules and how to apply for item RP29.

Item RP29 is paid at £29.50 per square metre (m²).
Item RP29 provides a self-supporting cover to an existing circular slurry or on-farm anaerobic digestate store, stopping rainwater from entering those stores.
Item RP29 improves air quality by reducing ammonia emissions.
Item RP29 may only be used with support from Catchment Sensitive Farming for roofing an existing above-ground circular store.
Item RP29 may only be used in areas targeted for the reduction of water and air pollution from agriculture.
Applicants must get advice from the Environment Agency to check what is required, as new and significantly altered stores may have to meet the SSAFO regulations 2010.
Item RP29 must not be used for floating covers.
Item RP29 must not be used to cover any structure other than a slurry or on-farm anaerobic digestate store.
Applicants must get relevant advice, consents or permits from the Environment Agency before starting any work under item RP29.
Applicants must contact their local planning authority to check if planning permission is needed before starting work under item RP29.
The grant holder must install a self-supporting cover with a minimum design life of 20 years (with maintenance).
The grant holder must install a rainproof and self-supporting cover, supported above the slurry or anaerobic digestate store and fastened by tensioners to the side of the tank.
The grant holder must ensure the tank can support the cover.
The grant holder must install the cover only when the store is empty.
The grant holder must direct rainwater away from the slurry or anaerobic digestate store into a clean water drain or rainwater harvesting system.
The grant holder must display appropriate warning signs to warn of the dangers of confined spaces and fragile covers.
The grant holder must meet the requirements of the SSAFO regulations 2010.
The grant holder must meet Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (NVZ) requirements where applicable.
The grant holder must keep written support from their Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser and provide this with their application.
The grant holder must keep photographs of the completed work and warning signs and provide them with their claim.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request receipted invoices or bank statements where a receipted invoice is unavailable.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request photographs of the site before works start.

The applicant must contact their local planning authority to check if planning permission is needed before starting work under item RP13.

The applicant must install 100mm or 150mm (internal diameter) drains.
The applicant must excavate trenches that are at least the width of the pipe's outside diameter plus 300mm.
The applicant must lay pipes on clay or sand that is at least 100mm thick.
The applicant must lay 100mm internal diameter pipes to a fall of no less than 1 in 60 to avoid settlement of solids or semi-solid material in water drains.
The applicant must lay 150mm internal diameter pipes to a fall of no less than 1 in 100 to avoid settlement of solids or semi-solid material in water drains.
The applicant must place inspection pits at no more than 90 metres apart in straight lines.
The applicant must only use uPVC pipes with sealed joints and join them according to the manufacturer's instructions.
The applicant must backfill the hole when the pipe is laid to at least 600mm in areas without traffic.
The applicant must backfill the hole when the pipe is laid to at least 900mm in areas with traffic.
The applicant must use compacted excavated material such as smooth stone, clay or sandy soil for backfill and remove any surplus.

Where it is not possible to backfill to 600mm (areas without traffic) or 900mm (areas with traffic), the applicant must wrap the pipes in plastic sheeting and encase them in at least 150mm of concrete.
The applicant must keep written support from their Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser and provide it with their application.

The applicant must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the site during the different stages of construction, or contracts, invoices or other documents confirming the technical specification for the completed works.
The applicant must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the completed works.
The applicant must keep and provide with their claim any consents or permissions connected with the work, including from the local planning authority.

Organic manures include slurry, poultry manures, solid manures (such as farmyard manure, sludge cake or compost), sewage sludge (also called biosolids), and other liquid manures (such as abattoir waste or anaerobic digestate).
Slurry is a liquid organic manure produced by livestock (other than poultry) while in a yard or building, and includes animal bedding and water that drains from areas where animals are kept.
A farmer must follow the NVZ organic manure storage guidance if any part of the farm buildings from which their slurry comes is within a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (NVZ).
A farmer in an NVZ must follow the guidance on the rules for using nitrogen fertilisers in NVZs and storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil.

A farmer must be able to store all the slurry they produce during the storage period, unless they reduce the volume by sending it off the farm or spreading some of it on fields with a low risk of runoff.

A farmer must be able to store all poultry manure produced in a yard or building during the storage period, unless they reduce the volume by sending it off the farm or spreading some of it on fields with a low risk of runoff.
The storage period for pigs and poultry runs from 1 October to 1 April inclusive (6 months).
The storage period for cattle, sheep, goats, deer and horses runs from 1 October to 1 March inclusive (5 months).
In addition to storing the slurry produced, a farmer must provide storage for any slurry, rainfall, washings or other liquid that enters the store during the storage period.
When calculating slurry storage capacity, a farmer must allow for wetter than average rainfall years (1 in 5 year).
A farmer may need a greater storage volume if in some years they cannot empty the store before the start of the closed period.
For new or substantially changed slurry storage, a farmer must also comply with the slurry storage rules.
A farmer may spread slurry or poultry manure on fields with a low risk of runoff to reduce the amount of storage capacity they need to provide.
If a farmer spreads slurry or poultry manure on low-risk runoff fields, they must still comply with the closed period for spreading slurry.
Land with a low risk of runoff has an average slope of less than 3 degrees.
Land with a low risk of runoff does not have land drains (other than a sealed pipe).
Land with a low risk of runoff is at least 50 metres from a watercourse or conduit leading to a watercourse (such as a ditch or working land drain).
A farmer must provide storage facilities for an additional 1 week's manure as a contingency measure in case they cannot spread on fields with a low risk of runoff on some days.

A farmer must store poultry manure, other types of solid organic manure, or animal bedding that contains organic manure in a container, on a waterproof base with runoff collection, in a roofed building, or in a temporary field heap.
A field heap must be at least 10 metres from any surface water (such as a river, pond or ditch) or land drain, or 30 metres if the land slopes at 12 degrees or more.
The location of a field heap must not be liable to being waterlogged or flooded.
Field heaps must be located at least 50 metres from a spring, well or borehole.
A farmer must move a field heap at least every 12 months.
A farmer must leave a 2-year gap before returning a field heap to the same site.
A farmer must keep a record of the sites used for field heaps and the dates of use.
A farmer must show all low-risk areas suitable for temporary field heaps on their risk map if they plan to use them.
Temporary field heaps must be made from manure solid enough to be stacked in a freestanding heap.
Temporary field heaps must not give rise to free drainage from within the stacked material.
Temporary field heaps must be covered with a waterproof material if they contain poultry manure without bedding or litter.
Temporary field heaps must occupy as small a surface area as is needed to support the weight of the heap without it collapsing.

If a farmer is constructing new storage for slurry, reconstructing part of a storage facility, or enlarging current storage facilities, they must follow the rules on storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil.
A farmer must record the capacity of the storage facilities on their farm and update any changes to this within 1 week of the change.
A farmer must record their calculation of the volume of manure that will be produced by the livestock they will keep in a building or on hard standing during the storage period.
A farmer must record their calculation showing the amount of storage they need.
A farmer must record any changes in volume and storage capacity due to introducing livestock onto the farm for the first time, within 1 month of the change.
Where relevant, a farmer must record their calculations to show how they have reduced their volume by sending slurry off the farm or spreading it on low-risk areas.
By 30 April each year, a farmer must record the numbers and type of livestock kept in buildings or on hard standing during the previous storage period.
By 30 April each year, a farmer must record the dates for the start and end of use and locations of any field sites used for storing solid manure.
A farmer does not need to record the start and end dates for their use of field sites separately if they record them on their risk map.
A farmer must keep storage records for 5 years.

Before starting any work, the applicant must get advice from the Environment Agency to check what is required, as new and significantly altered stores must meet the silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil (SSAFO) regulations 2010.

Water companies are investing £4.6 billion in environmental improvements in the five-year period of 2020 to 2025.
Environmental improvement schemes agreed through Ofwat's 2019 Price Review are delivered through changes to environmental permits regulating discharges to the water environment.
Environmental improvement schemes agreed through Ofwat's 2019 Price Review are delivered through abstraction licences regulating taking of water.

Five water companies — Severn Trent Water, South Staffs Water, South West Water, Thames Water and United Utilities — will invest an extra £793 million on top of their existing Water Industry National Environment Plan packages.
Water companies must plan a minimum of 25 years in advance to make sure that there is enough water to meet future demands.
Water companies must improve their performance, which has stagnated in recent years.
Some water companies have not achieved their statutory obligations, such as reducing leakage and reducing pollution from storm sewer overflows.

The Environment Agency exercises its permitting and enforcement powers under the Environmental Permitting Regulations 2016 to ensure water company discharges and abstractions meet environmental objectives for water bodies.

The Environment Agency exercises its permitting and enforcement powers under the Water Resources Act 1991 to ensure water company discharges and abstractions meet environmental objectives for water bodies.
Farmers must not pollute the water environment.
Farmers must comply with the Farming Rules for Water.
The Farming Rules for Water apply to manufactured fertiliser and all organic manures, including farm manure and slurry, sewage sludge (biosolids), and imported waste organic materials spread to land.
The Environment Agency exercises its powers under the Farming Rules for Water as part of the programmes of measures.

Where farmers go beyond the basic requirements of the Farming Rules for Water, the government will fund additional environmental outcomes through schemes such as Countryside Stewardship and the new environmental land management schemes.
Through environmental land management schemes, farmers and other land managers may enter into agreements to be paid for delivering improved water quality.
Through environmental land management schemes, farmers and other land managers may enter into agreements to be paid for delivering habitat protection and creation.
Through environmental land management schemes, farmers and other land managers may enter into agreements to be paid for delivering natural flood management and carbon capture.
Legislation provides for mandatory biodiversity net gain to ensure that new developments, including housing, benefit biodiversity by creating and improving habitats and local green spaces.
Developers will be expected to deliver habitat creation or enhancement as part of delivering mandatory 10% biodiversity net gain, to be maintained for at least 30 years.
The Environment Act 2021 introduces Local Nature Recovery Strategies to drive the development of the Nature Recovery Network.
Each Local Nature Recovery Strategy must agree priorities for nature's recovery for the area it covers.
Each Local Nature Recovery Strategy must map the most valuable existing habitat for nature for the area it covers.
Each Local Nature Recovery Strategy must map specific proposals for creating or improving habitat for nature and wider environmental goals for the area it covers.

The government has committed to reform local flood risk management planning so that every area of England will have a more strategic and comprehensive plan that drives long-term local action and investment by 2026.

Future local flood risk management plans should support a catchment-based approach, considering all sources of flood risk and the potential for action across the whole of an area, upstream and downstream.
All English water companies have signed up to a public interest commitment with specific 2030 commitments on carbon and leakage reduction, as well as plastics, social mobility and water poverty.
The Government has committed more than £750 million to England's Nature for Climate Fund.
Net zero and climate change adaptation will be supported through environmental land management schemes.

The Courtauld Commitment 2025 aims to reduce food waste, cut carbon and protect critical water resources by bringing together organisations across the food system to make food and drink production and consumption more sustainable.

Low carbon technologies and fuels, such as ground source heating and cooling, biomass crops, low carbon hydrogen, and carbon capture and storage, must be compatible with the environmental objectives of the river basin management plans.

The Environment Agency carries out onsite inspections to assess compliance with water company wastewater discharge permits.
The Environment Agency's target for onsite water company inspections in 2025 to 2026 is 10,000 inspections.
By the end of quarter 3 (31 December 2025), the Environment Agency completed 7,906 water company onsite inspections against a quarter 3 target of 7,000, representing 113% performance.
There were 111 water company wastewater treatment works and water treatment works identified as high risk failing sites in the previous year.
Of the 111 high risk failing water company sites, 84 (76%) are currently meeting the requirements of the sewage treatment works compliance measure as of quarter 3 2025 to 2026.
A high risk failing water company site meets the sewage treatment works compliance requirement if it has returned to compliance.

A high risk failing water company site meets the sewage treatment works compliance requirement if it has submitted a compliance action plan (CAP) to the Environment Agency that clearly outlines the steps and timelines for achieving compliance.

A high risk failing water company site meets the sewage treatment works compliance requirement if the Environment Agency has started enforcement action alongside other regulatory work to bring the site back into compliance.

Farm inspections (excluding permitted intensive pig and poultry units) assess compliance with legislation covering slurry, silage, agricultural fuel oil storage, farming rules for water, and nitrate vulnerable zone regulations.

Approximately 10% of the Environment Agency's farm inspections are remote, using earth observation techniques.

LTIFR captures any work-related incidents (both harm and ill-health) that result in a colleague being off work for more than one day.

The Environment Agency's 2025 to 2026 workforce diversity target for one measure is 50%.

The Environment Agency carries out onsite inspections to assess compliance with water company wastewater discharge permits.
The Environment Agency has an annual target of 10,000 onsite inspections of water companies for 2025 to 2026.
By the end of quarter 2 (2025 to 2026), the Environment Agency completed 5,228 water company onsite inspections against a target of 4,500, a performance of 116%.
The Environment Agency provides a training programme for newly recruited water industry regulation officers to ensure skills and capacity for high quality inspection delivery.
111 water company sites were identified as high risk failing sites in 2024 to 2025.
Of the 111 high risk failing water company sites, 107 (96%) are currently meeting the requirements of the sewage treatment works compliance measure.
A water company wastewater treatment site meets the compliance measure if it has returned to compliance.

A water company wastewater treatment site meets the compliance measure if it has submitted a compliance action plan (CAP) to the Environment Agency that clearly outlines the steps and timelines for achieving compliance.
A water company wastewater treatment site meets the compliance measure if the Environment Agency has started enforcement action alongside other regulatory work to bring the site back into compliance.
A high risk failing water company site may change from 'meeting' to 'not meeting' the compliance measure if it does not comply with the steps in its action plan within the agreed timescales.
A high risk failing water company site may change from 'meeting' to 'not meeting' the compliance measure if it has another serious permit non-compliance during the year.

Farm inspections assess compliance with relevant legislation, mainly focusing on slurry, silage, agricultural fuel oil storage, farming rules for water, and nitrate vulnerable zone regulations.
The Environment Agency has an annual target of 4,000 farm inspections for the 2025 to 2026 financial year.
At the end of quarter 2 of 2025 to 2026, the Environment Agency had completed 2,037 farm inspections.
Approximately 10% of the Environment Agency's farm inspections are conducted remotely using earth observation techniques.

The Environment Agency has received a new bespoke application for an environmental permit from Paul George under the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016.
The application reference EPR/XP3124SH/A001 is a new bespoke DfR (Slurry Pit) permit application for Nansmerrow Farm, Truro TR2 4AP.
Comments on the application must be sent by 11th February 2025.
Comments on the application may be submitted online via Citizen Space.
Comments on the application may be submitted via email to pscpublicresponse@environment-agency.gov.uk.
The Environment Agency will normally place any comments it receives on the public register, including the respondent's name but not their personal contact details.

Farmers and land managers may apply through an Environmental Land Management (ELM) scheme to farm productively in a way that benefits the environment.
There are 3 ELM schemes and supporting capital items.
SFI (Sustainable Farming Incentive) is closed for applications.
SFI pays farmers and land managers to take up or maintain sustainable farming and land management practices that protect and benefit the environment.
SFI pays farmers and land managers to take up or maintain sustainable farming and land management practices that support food production.
SFI pays farmers and land managers to take up or maintain sustainable farming and land management practices that improve productivity.
There will be 2 application windows for the SFI 2026 offer.
SFI 2026 Window 1 opens from June 2026 for small farms and farms without an existing ELM revenue agreement.
SFI 2026 Window 2 opens from September 2026 for all farms.
The Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) scheme opens in September 2025, with grant values that vary depending on land management actions included in the CSHT agreement.
CSHT pays farmers and land managers to manage land in a way that protects, restores or enhances the environment.
CSHT pays farmers and land managers to manage land in a way that mitigates the effects of climate change.
CSHT can be applied on woodland, farmed land, land managed for nature, or a combination of these, including SSSIs, commons, woodlands, scheduled monuments.
Natural England or the Forestry Commission (or both) have been providing relevant pre-application advice from January 2025 to enable farmers and land managers to prepare a CSHT application.
A farmer or land manager must not apply for CSHT until they receive an invitation to get pre-application advice from Natural England or the Forestry Commission.
Landscape Recovery pays groups of farmers and land managers to do long-term, large-scale projects together.
Land eligible for Landscape Recovery must be in England and consist of at least 500 connected hectares.
Capital Grants will open in July 2026.
Farmers and land managers may apply for grants through the Farming Investment Fund (FIF) to invest in new technology, equipment and infrastructure.
The Farming Equipment and Technology Fund (FETF) has 3 grants to help buy items to improve productivity, manage slurry, and improve animal health and welfare.
The Farming Equipment and Technology Fund (FETF) provides a grant value of between £1,000 and £25,000 for each grant theme, and is now closed.

A total budget of £50 million is available to farmers, growers, foresters and their contractors through the Farming Investment Fund, comprising £20 million for equipment to boost farm productivity, £20 million to improve animal health and welfare, and £10 million for slurry management.

Farmers and land managers within an area of outstanding natural beauty or national park in England may apply for funding through the Farming in Protected Landscapes (FiPL) programme at any time of year, with projects required to end by March 2029.
FiPL projects must end by March 2029.
To be eligible for FiPL funding, a farmer or land manager must be planning projects that support nature recovery.
To be eligible for FiPL funding, a farmer or land manager must be planning projects that mitigate the impacts of climate change.
To be eligible for FiPL funding, a farmer or land manager must be planning projects that provide opportunities for people to discover nature.
To be eligible for FiPL funding, a farmer or land manager must be planning projects that protect or improve the quality and character of the landscape.
Keepers of chickens, ducks, geese or turkeys can get funding for up to 3 biosecurity reviews on each of their poultry sites, at £430 per review, open at any time of year until 31 March 2029.

Farmers who keep cattle, sheep or pigs can get funding for a vet to visit their farm to carry out an animal health and welfare review and an endemic disease follow-up, at £258 to £1,844 per review or follow-up depending on livestock type.
Egg production farmers may apply for a grant to add a veranda onto, or upgrade or replace, existing laying hen or pullet housing to improve animal health and welfare; applications are now closed.
The England Woodland Creation Grant (EWCO) provides funding to create new woodland on areas that are at least 1 hectare.
EWCO provides a grant value of up to £10,200 per hectare, with an additional £12,700 per hectare if the woodland delivers wider benefits to society, nature recovery and the environment.
New woodland eligible for EWCO funding must cover an area of at least 1 hectare.
The Woodland Creation Planning Grant (WCPG) provides funding to prepare a Woodland Creation Design Plan that complies with the UK Forestry Standard, with a maximum grant value of £30,500 per project.
A Woodland Creation Design Plan funded through the WCPG must comply with the UK Forestry Standard.
The Woodland Tree Health (WTH) grant pays landowners to restock or improve woodland after tree health problems.
Farmers and land managers may apply for grants through the Farming Innovation Programme (FIP) to improve agricultural and horticultural productivity, sustainability and resilience.
Farmers and land managers may apply for grants through the Farming Innovation Programme (FIP) to reduce the environmental impact of agriculture and horticulture.

Farmers and land managers may apply for grants through the Farming Innovation Programme (FIP) to use science and research to develop solutions for practical challenges in agriculture and horticulture.
Delinked payments replaced Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) payments in 2024.
The 2023 scheme year was the last year of the Basic Payment Scheme (BPS).
Delinked payments will be made each year from 2024 until 2027.
Capital payment rates for Countryside Stewardship changed from 5 January 2023.
An Agreement Holder must return the signed acceptance declaration to RPA within 20 working days of the date on the Agreement Offer letter.
If an Agreement Holder does not accept the Agreement Offer in time, RPA will withdraw it.
An Agreement Holder may only modify, extend or amend the agreement with RPA's consent.
An Agreement Holder must keep all records relevant to the expenditure of the grant for at least 7 years from the end of the agreement.
An Agreement Holder must obtain and keep evidence to show that all requirements of the agreement have been carried out to support a claim or to support an environmental outcome site visit.
An Agreement Holder must keep evidence that they are eligible for the Countryside Stewardship scheme.
An Agreement Holder must keep evidence to show that they, their business, their land or capital items are eligible, as they may have to show this if chosen for an environmental outcome site visit.
An Agreement Holder must keep all invoices, receipts, accounts and any other relevant documentation relating to the expenditure of the grant for at least 7 years from the end of the agreement.
It is the Agreement Holder's responsibility to obtain all consents, approvals or permissions required due to their specific circumstances and to carry out the particular capital item.
Consents and permissions obtained by the Agreement Holder must remain effective, and records of them must be kept, for the duration of the agreement.
For capital items requiring dated photographic evidence, an Agreement Holder must have this evidence available on request.
For a partial or full claim, the Agreement Holder must take a dated photograph after the works have been completed and send it with the payment claim showing the 'works completed condition'.
The 'before work' and 'works completed' photographs must be taken from the same position.
Photographs submitted as evidence must be in focus and clearly show the relevant capital item or environmental feature.
Digital photographs submitted by email must be sent as JPEG files.
Digital images submitted as evidence should not be smaller than 600 x 400 pixels.
Printed photographs submitted as evidence must be no smaller than 15 cm x 10 cm.
The Agreement Holder must comply with all instructions and guidance relating to acknowledging and publicising the support provided under the agreement.

If an Agreement Holder is unable to meet requirements due to Force Majeure or exceptional circumstances, they must write to inform RPA within 8 weeks from the date on which they (or any person authorised to act for them) are able to do so.
An Agreement Holder must not start work (including ordering and paying for materials) before the agreement start date.
An Agreement Holder must keep invoices, receipts, accounts and all other relevant financial documentation for at least 7 years from the end date of the agreement.
Financial documentation relating to the agreement must be dated on or after the agreement start date.
An Agreement Holder must complete all capital works within 2 years of the agreement start date.
RPA must receive all claims for payment no later than 3 months after the agreement end date.
An Agreement Holder may only submit a claim after payment for all items or activities being claimed has left their bank account and all works have been carried out.

When using own labour or a farm employee's labour for capital works, the Agreement Holder must prepare time sheets signed by the employee and employer showing the hourly rate, work carried out, and the date the work was done.

An Agreement Holder must keep time sheet records relating to their own labour or a farm employee's labour in either paper or electronic form for the full period of the agreement and for at least 7 years after the agreement has ended.
An Agreement Holder must produce time sheet records if RPA asks for them.
An Agreement Holder must tell employees about the agreement and its requirements.
It is the Agreement Holder's responsibility to ensure that work carried out using own labour or a farm employee's labour does not breach the terms of the agreement.
If an employee commits any breaches of the agreement, the Agreement Holder will be responsible for any resulting payment reductions.
An Agreement Holder must tell contractors about the agreement and its requirements.
It is the Agreement Holder's responsibility to ensure that work carried out by contractors does not breach the terms of the agreement.
If a contractor commits any breaches of the agreement, the Agreement Holder will be responsible for any resulting payment reductions.

An Agreement Holder must keep records of work carried out by contractors in either paper or electronic form for the full period of the agreement and for at least 7 years after the agreement has ended.
An Agreement Holder must produce contractor records if RPA asks for them.
The minimum claim value for a CS Capital Grants payment claim is £500, except the minimum does not apply to a final interim claim.
If an Agreement Holder is using a contractor, they must pay the contractor before claiming payment from RPA.
RPA will pay valid claims within 2 months of receiving them.
An Agreement Holder must keep evidence to show that the capital works are finished.
The Agreement Holder must keep capital items to the condition and specification for which the aid was granted for 5 years from the start date of the agreement.
An Agreement Holder must allow any UK public authority (or their authorised representatives or auditors) to access their land or premises to carry out site visits.
An Agreement Holder must help and co-operate with any person carrying out a site visit.

Refusal to co-operate with or obstruction of a site visit will be treated as a breach of the Countryside Stewardship terms and conditions and may result in recovery, suspension or termination of the agreement.
If an Agreement Holder does not provide records when asked, or there are discrepancies in records, RPA will treat this as a breach of the agreement.
If an Agreement Holder receives Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) payments, they must follow the cross compliance rules.

An Agreement Holder who does not receive BPS payments or annual revenue payments through any other agreement for Countryside Stewardship, Environmental Stewardship, or Farm Woodland Payments under the English Woodland Grant scheme does not need to follow the cross compliance rules.
If an Agreement Holder does not meet the terms of their agreement, RPA may reduce or withhold payment or ask for repayment of monies already paid.
In cases of fraud or severe non-compliance, RPA can withhold all support and stop new Countryside Stewardship applications and applications to other DEFRA grant schemes for up to 2 years.
If an Agreement Holder submits a claim for more than the value of eligible costs, RPA will reduce the payment to the correct amount.
An Agreement Holder cannot transfer their agreement during the 2-year agreement period.

If an Agreement Holder sells or lets all or part of the land under their CS Capital Grants agreement to another party, RPA will end the agreement on those parcels and the Agreement Holder may need to repay all or part of the grant payments already received.
Some capital items are only available in a Countryside Stewardship High Priority Area for Water or Air Quality and with the support and approval of a Catchment Sensitive Farming Officer (CSFO).
The capital items an applicant applies for must address the water or air pollutants identified as affecting that catchment or area in order to receive support and approval from a CSFO.
An applicant must clearly show how water pollutants are currently reaching a watercourse or underground aquifer in order to receive CSFO support and approval.
An applicant must identify where ammonia emissions are coming from in order to receive CSFO support and approval for air quality capital items.
An applicant must show how the proposed work will prevent losses of water pollutants or ammonia emissions in order to receive CSFO support and approval.
An applicant must show that they have included a suitable range of capital items to prevent the pollutant reaching a watercourse or the atmosphere.
Where work needs to be carried out over a number of years, an applicant must agree with their CSFO how the work is phased.
Applications that meet local water and air objectives will receive a higher score.
An applicant must have approval from a local CSFO to apply for certain capital items listed in the CSFO-approval table.
If an applicant applies for CSFO-approval capital items without having CSFO approval, those items will be removed from their application.
An applicant's land must be in a Countryside Stewardship High Priority Area for Water or Air Quality in order to apply for CSFO-approval capital items.
An applicant whose land is in a Countryside Stewardship High Priority Area for Water or Air must contact their local CSFO at least 6 weeks before they intend to submit their application.
If an applicant does not allow at least 6 weeks for the CSFO to review their request, there is no guarantee that the request will be considered.
An applicant must complete the 'Request for approval from a Catchment Sensitive Farming Officer' form and send it to their local CSFO to request support.
For parcels to be included in a Countryside Stewardship application, they must be registered on the Rural Payments service.
If an applicant needs new land parcel numbers, they must fill in an RLE1 form and send it with sketch maps.
There are legal requirements in English law around certain farming activities to protect people, livestock and the environment.
Government agencies and local authorities inspect farms and land to make sure farmers and land managers follow the rules.
Poultry keepers must register to keep poultry.
Keepers of cattle, bison and buffalo must comply with the rules for keeping cattle, bison and buffalo in England and Wales.
Landowners must protect the public from cattle kept on their land.
Keepers of sheep and goats must comply with the rules for keeping sheep and goats in England.
Pig keepers must comply with the rules for keeping pigs in England.
Deer keepers in England must comply with tagging, movement and record keeping rules for keeping deer.
Farmers and land managers must protect animal welfare on farm and in transport.
Farmers and land managers must produce food that is safe to eat for humans or animals.
Farmers and land managers must report notifiable diseases in animals.
Cattle keepers in England must get their cattle tested for bovine TB.
Farmers must use animal medicines responsibly on the farm.
Operators of intensive pig and poultry farms must hold the required environmental permits.
Farmers and land managers must ensure that animal activities do not damage soil.
Farmers and land managers must store organic manures in accordance with the rules for nitrate vulnerable zones.
Farmers and land managers must store slurry, silage and agricultural fuel oil safely.
Farmers and land managers must dispose of fallen stock and dead animals safely.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the rules to prevent pollution when storing agricultural fuel oil.
Farmers and land managers must manage farming and horticultural activities to prevent soil erosion and water pollution.
Landowners must comply with their responsibilities in relation to public rights of way.
Land managers must comply with the management requirements and rights and responsibilities relating to open access land.
Land managers must comply with the management requirements and rights and responsibilities relating to coastal margin land.
Owners or occupiers of sites of special scientific interest must manage those sites in accordance with the applicable rules.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the rules on heather and grass burning and must obtain a licence where required.
Farmers and land managers must check whether they need to apply for an Environmental Impact Assessment before changing or improving rural land.
Professional users of plant protection products must register as a professional user.
Farmers and land managers must use, transport and store pesticides safely.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the legal requirements set out in the code of practice for using plant protection products.
Farmers and land managers must plan their use of fertiliser or manure to reduce water pollution.
Farmers and land managers in nitrate vulnerable zones must comply with the rules when using fertilisers or storing manure.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the rules when using sewage sludge on their land.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the waste exemption rules when spreading waste to benefit their land.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the restrictions and rules on burning crop residues.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the rules on plant breeders' rights, national listing of plant varieties, seed certification and farm saved seed.
Landowners must comply with the countryside hedgerow protection rules before removing a hedgerow.
Farmers and land managers must comply with hedgerow management rules when cutting and trimming hedgerows.
Farmers and land managers must comply with hedgerow management rules regarding buffer strips.
Farmers and land managers must obtain permission before carrying out work on scheduled monuments.
Farmers and land managers must check whether they need to apply for an Environmental Impact Assessment before planning woodland projects.
Farmers and land managers must maintain watercourses and understand the permissions needed to work around them.
Farmers and land managers must check whether they need a licence to abstract water.
Farmers and land managers must comply with the rules for managing hazardous waste, such as chemical containers and sheep dip.
Farmers and land managers making discharges to surface water or groundwater must hold the required environmental permits.
Farmers and land managers using sheep dip must comply with the groundwater protection code for sheep dip.
Farmers and land managers working with materials that come from animals but are not for human consumption must comply with the rules for the animal by-products industry.
All permitted intensive pig and poultry farms are inspected.
The Environment Agency checks all structures on the farm, including animal housing, oil and chemical storage, feed storage, carcass storage, foot dips, drainage systems, and slurry or manure stores.
Farm records are inspected, including training records.
Farm records are inspected, including the complaints procedure.
Farm records are inspected, including records of energy use, raw materials, water use, waste and carcass disposal.
Farm records are inspected, including livestock numbers.
Farm records are inspected, including feed dockets.
Farm records are inspected, including manure export records and soil and manure nutrient analysis.
Farm plans are inspected, including the accident management plan.
Farm plans are inspected, including the inspection and maintenance plan.
Farm plans are inspected, including the odour management plan.
Farm plans are inspected, including the noise management plan.
Farm plans are inspected, including the manure management plan.
A certification body appointed under the Pig and Poultry Assurance Scheme may inspect a farm, and such inspections are very similar to the Environment Agency inspection.
Members of the Pig and Poultry Assurance Scheme are inspected once a year over a 3-year period.
For members of the Pig and Poultry Assurance Scheme, the Environment Agency carries out one of the three annual inspections and a certification body carries out the other two.
For farms not in the Pig and Poultry Assurance Scheme, the frequency of inspection depends on the risk of pollution from the farm.
Intensive pig and poultry farm operators receive up to 14 days' notice of an inspection.
An inspection of an intensive pig or poultry farm usually takes 2 hours.
The Environment Agency sends the farm operator a Compliance Assessment Report setting out any actions they need to take following an inspection.
The Environment Agency carries out a follow-up visit to an intensive pig or poultry farm if the operator has serious problems meeting the standards.
An intensive farming permit applicant must request an ammonia screening assessment from the Environment Agency before applying for a new intensive farming permit.
An intensive farming permit applicant must request an ammonia screening assessment from the Environment Agency before applying to change (vary) an existing permit that may affect ammonia emissions.
An intensive farming permit applicant must complete the relevant ammonia screening assessment data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must complete an online ammonia screening assessment request form.
A permit holder does not need to complete an ammonia screening assessment when varying their intensive farming permit in a way that will not affect ammonia emissions from the site.
A permit holder does not need to complete an ammonia screening assessment when transferring or surrendering their permit.
There is no charge for the ammonia screening assessment service.
Permitted intensive farms must meet the standards set out in the BAT Reference Document (BREF) for the Intensive Rearing of Poultry or Pigs (IRPP).
An intensive farming permit applicant must confirm in their proposals that all livestock housing meets Best Available Techniques (BAT).

An intensive farming permit applicant must demonstrate that new housing (new plant) will meet the BAT associated emissions levels (AELs) for ammonia emissions and nitrogen and phosphorus excretion, where applicable.
An intensive farming permit applicant must submit information with their application to confirm the proposed housing systems will meet the relevant ammonia BAT associated emissions levels (AELs).
An intensive farming permit applicant must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms including a breakdown of livestock numbers by type and by housing.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include ventilation system type (fan or natural) in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include ventilation location (roof or side mounted) in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include the height of roof vents and the fan airflow speed in metres per second (if fans are roof mounted) in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include the amount of manure stored on the farm at any one time, in tonnes, in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include the surface area (in square metres) of slurry storage facilities on the site, including lagoons, in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include the type of cover used on slurry storage (if present) in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must include the most appropriate ammonia emission factors for the livestock and housing type in the data spreadsheet forms.
An intensive farming permit applicant must save the completed spreadsheet form and upload it when submitting the online ammonia screening request form.

After the Environment Agency completes an ammonia screening assessment request, they will notify the applicant whether a detailed modelling assessment of ammonia emissions is required to support the application.
An intensive farming permit applicant must submit their intensive farming permit application within 6 months of their ammonia screening assessment to ensure the data remains current.
A permit holder must hold SR2010 No 4 to use waste code 02 01 01 (soils and sludges from washing and cleaning fruit and vegetables).
Manure and slurry produced in agriculture are not waste when used as a fertiliser.
An agricultural operator does not need an environmental permit or waste exemption to store and spread manure and slurry produced in agriculture onto agricultural land.
The exemption from needing a permit for manure and slurry applies even when used by agricultural premises that did not produce the manure and slurry.
Waste code 02 01 06 applies to manure and slurry that is not produced in agriculture, for example from riding stables, equine centres, kennels, farms for ornamental fish, and animal parks.
Waste code 02 01 06 also applies to manure and slurry intended for spreading on non-agricultural land.

For non-plant tissue bedding such as gypsum, the operator must use waste code 02 01 99 (Slurry and manure and soiled bedding from any premises except abattoirs, soiled biodegradable bedding not made from plant tissue, soiled bedding desiccants only).
Landspreading soiled bedding containing waste bedding materials must meet environmental controls under the Environmental Permitting Regulations.
A permit holder must not treat waste under the mobile plant for landspreading standard rules permits and deployment.
A permit holder must not spread waste code 02 01 06 (farmyard manure and slurry) to land if other livestock have access to it.
A permit holder must not allow livestock to graze on land on which poultry litter has been spread for a minimum of 5 weeks.
Agricultural manure and slurry mixed with non-waste bedding materials such as straw are category 2 animal by-products controlled by the Animal By-Products (Enforcement) (England) Regulations.
A permit holder must be able to meet all the conditions and register the T23 Aerobic composting exemption and U10 waste exemption.
For grass leys, forage crops and permanent pasture, the operator must inject liquid and solid odorous wastes (02 02 series) into the soil.
After injecting odorous wastes on grassland, the operator must wait a minimum of 3 weeks before using the land for grazing or cutting.
For arable land, the permit holder must surface spread odorous liquid and solid wastes and then cultivate to incorporate the waste into the soil on stubble or bare land within 24 hours.
For the waste to be spread, the permit holder must consider the statutory guidance 'Food and drink waste hierarchy: deal with surplus and waste'.
The waste producer must treat blood following the animal by-products regulations before it can be applied to land.
The permit holder must make sure that blood has been treated following the animal by-products regulations before applying it to land.
The permit holder must take precautions to minimise visual and odour nuisance when applying blood waste to land.
The permit holder must provide information on the chemicals that were used to treat blood in their waste analysis.
The permit holder must ensure that all boluses found in gut contents have been removed at the abattoir before landspreading.
The permit holder should aim to landspread stored gut material within 24 hours.
After landspreading gut contents on grassland, the permit holder must wait a minimum of 3 weeks before using the land for grazing or cutting.
If the permit holder plans to spread processed animal protein, they must follow the Animal By-Products Regulations.
Processed animal protein waste (02 02 99) must be incorporated into the soil within 24 hours or sooner.
The permit holder must not spread processed animal protein waste on grassland where stock may eat surface material.
The permit holder must not spread processed animal protein waste on grassland before cutting it for silage or hay.
The permit holder must cover stockpiles of processed animal protein waste to prevent scavengers, leachate generation, flies and dust nuisance.
The permit holder must keep stockpiles of processed animal protein waste away from land drainage, surface water, boreholes, groundwater source protection zones and sensitive human receptors.
Lairage waste must not contain anything other than livestock excreta and bedding materials.
Lairage waste is a category 2 animal by-product.
The permit holder must consider whether they will be allowed to apply lairage waste during notifiable animal disease outbreaks to prevent disease transmission.
The permit holder must make sure that any animal by-product waste has had the correct treatment and is suitable for landspreading.

To minimise risks to animal and human health, wash waters (waste code 02 02 99) must be applied to land by subsurface injection to a depth of at least 15cm, or by shallower injection or surface application worked into the soil before allowing grazing animals on the land.
The permit holder must follow the guidance in rule 3.3: odour in Landspreading: how to comply with your permit when spreading odorous 02 03 01 wastes.
The permit holder must take care if landspreading wastes that are low in dry matter at high rates and when soil conditions are not suitable.
Soils from sugar beet washing may be applied at a rate of 1,500 t/ha, subject to the restriction on maximum allowed plant nutrient loading.
The permit holder must base the liming benefit of lime sugar sludge on the specific neutralising value of the waste.
Dairy wastes produced on site and stored in silos may provide an environment for bacteria to increase, which may affect livestock grazing on recently spread land or make the wastes more odorous.
Under the U10 waste exemption, the permit holder must dilute milk with an equal amount or more of water or slurry before spreading it.
Once milk is moved off the farm or the dairy it came from, the animal by-products regulations apply.
The permit holder must not apply brewing waste (02 07 01) to land with a soil pH of less than 5.5.
The permit holder must not landspread distillery wash water where the soil pH is less than 5.5, unless the waste producer has added caustic soda or a similar alkali material to neutralise the waste.
The permit holder must know the pH of Kieselguhr sludge waste before storing or landspreading it.
The permit holder must not accept Kieselguhr sludge waste unless the waste producer has adjusted the pH back to pH 6.0 to 7.0.
The permit holder must not mix Kieselguhr sludge waste to adjust the pH, as mixing is treatment and treatment is not allowed under SR2010 No 4.
The permit holder must not apply non-alcoholic drinks waste (02 07 05) to land with soils with a pH of less than 5.5, unless the producer has neutralised the acidity of the waste.
The permit holder must follow rule 3.3 odour in Landspreading: how to comply with your permit when managing trub and yeast waste odour.
After applying fatty waste (02 02 series) to agricultural grassland, the permit holder must leave an interval of at least 3 weeks before using the grass for grazing or cutting.
The permit holder must make sure any fatty material is thoroughly mixed through the soil profile during field operations when injecting fatty waste.
The Environment Agency corporate scorecard is reported every 3 months to executive directors and the board within the Environment Agency.
The Environment Agency Corporate Scorecard is shared with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) and is published publicly.
The Environment Agency Corporate Scorecard measures contribute towards the wider Defra single departmental plan.
Green on the red-amber-green system means the Environment Agency is performing at or above the target set.
Amber on the red-amber-green system means the Environment Agency is falling slightly short of the target.
Red on the red-amber-green system means there are improvements to be made by the Environment Agency.
The Environment Agency and its partners enhanced a further 538km of the water environment in quarter two 2019/20, bringing the total to 1,489km for 2019/20.
A total of 6,479km of the water environment has been enhanced since the publication of the 2015 updated river basin management plans.
The Environment Agency undertook works to protect 862km of water bodies from deterioration in quarter two 2019/20.
Quarterly incident data shown in the corporate scorecard is initial data which is subject to change.
Results for the most recent quarters of pollution incident recording are likely to increase in future reports due to inherent lag time in investigating and recording all necessary incident details.
95 serious pollution incidents were recorded for quarter two 2019/20 at initial reporting stage, compared with 147 at the same stage in the previous year.
The target for serious pollution incidents for the year 2019/20 is no more than 400.
In quarter two 2019/20, five pollution incidents were attributed to agriculture, significantly less than in the last five years.
Four of the five agriculture-related pollution incidents in quarter two 2019/20 were slurry-related, caused by poor, insufficient or faulty storage.
There were nine illegal waste pollution incidents in quarter two 2019/20, all of which were category two, representing the second lowest number of such incidents recorded since 2015.
Of the nine illegal waste incidents in quarter two 2019/20, three have been recommended for prosecution, two for warning letters and one for a formal caution.
Water company pollution incidents in quarter two 2019/20 decreased slightly from 19 in quarter one to 18 in quarter two.
There were 21 pollution incidents from Other Non-Regulated sectors in quarter two 2019/20, compared to 62 for the same quarter of 2018/19.
30 separate flood risk projects better protected 5,170 homes in quarter two of 2019/20, bringing the cumulative total since April 2015 to 200,425.
The Environment Agency forecasts cumulative flood risk delivery at the end of 2019/20 to be over 250,000 homes better protected against a target of 240,000.
The flood risk management programme remains on track to better protect 300,000 homes by the end of March 2021.
The national position for KPI 962 Environment Agency high consequence flood and coastal risk management assets has reduced to 96.9%, below the national target of 98%.

Where the performance of a flood or coastal risk management asset is reduced, the Environment Agency will take action to ensure that flood risk is effectively managed until the asset is fully repaired or replaced.
The Environment Agency estimates that 6,460 trained and capable incident response staff are ready to respond to incidents, representing 108% of the target of 6,000.
The Environment Agency maintains a pool of around 800 people in training who will contribute to its trained and ready incident response workforce.
An additional 200 staff from core Defra support the Environment Agency's communications during incidents.
Planning performance for quarter two 2019/20 is 95.5% of planning applications determined in line with Environment Agency advice, against a target of 97%, and is therefore rated amber.
In quarter two 2019/20, 43 planning applications were not determined in line with Environment Agency advice, 10 of which were for major development.
Local planning authorities not insisting on flood risk assessments when required is contrary to government planning policy.
The Environment Agency invested 43% of its full year budget in the first half of 2019/20, amounting to £574m compared to £558m in the same period of 2018/19.
The Environment Agency's carbon dioxide emissions for mid-year 2019/20 are 9% under the mid-year target.
The Environment Agency's 2020 carbon dioxide emissions target is 30,876 tonnes.
The proportion of hybrid vehicles in the Environment Agency fleet increased to 31% by the end of quarter two 2019/20, compared to 16% at the end of quarter two in the previous year.
15 new BAME recruits were hired in quarter two 2019/20, representing 5.7% of the 264 new external starters, compared to a 2020 BAME workforce target of 14%.
The proportion of BAME employees in the Environment Agency remained at 4.3% in quarter two 2019/20, against a 2016/20 target of 14%.
