The percentage of female Executive Managers in the Environment Agency is 41.1% in quarter two 2019/20, compared to a target of 50%.
Lost Time Incidents are defined by the Environment Agency as work-related injuries resulting in a day or more of lost time.
The Environment Agency's Lost Time Incident Frequency Rate target is a ceiling of 0.11 injuries per 100,000 hours worked.
In quarter two 2019/20 the Environment Agency's Lost Time Incident Frequency Rate remained the same as quarter one, representing a significant improvement on the previous year.
The number of active high risk illegal waste sites increased from 260 to 264 in quarter two 2019/20, against a profiled target of 223.
On average it is taking the Environment Agency 237 days to stop an illegal waste site, despite 49% of new sites being stopped in less than 90 days.
Waste exemption T24 is in charging band 1.
The anaerobic digestion process produces biogas, which can be burnt to generate energy for use on the farm or for export to the National Grid.
Under exemption T24, a holder may carry out anaerobic digestion of manure and plant tissue waste in a dedicated AD plant to produce a digestate.
Under exemption T24, a holder may burn the biogas to produce energy to provide power on the farm or to export to the National Grid.
Under exemption T24, a holder may carry out sorting, screening, cutting, shredding, pulverising and chipping of waste to assist the AD process.
Under exemption T24, a holder must not dispose of biogas by flaring, except when appliances burning biogas break down or are being maintained.
Under exemption T24, a holder must not dispose of biogas by venting it to the atmosphere.
Under exemption T24, a holder must not compost the manure and plant tissue waste.
Under exemption T24, a holder must not spread the digestate produced under this exemption.
Waste treated under exemption T24 must fit within the relevant waste code and description listed in the List of Wastes (LoW) Regulations.
Under exemption T24, a holder may store or treat up to 1,250 cubic metres of waste at any one time.
The 1,250 cubic metre storage limit does not include manure and slurry pits where those materials have been produced on the same farm and are being stored before being treated in the digester.
Where manure and slurry is imported from other farms and stored before being fed into the AD plant, that storage is included within the 1,250 cubic metre limit.
Where manure and slurry is mixed with plant tissue waste, the 1,250 cubic metre limit includes the storage of plant tissue waste, material in the digester, and the storage of the resulting digestate.
Under exemption T24, the exemption holder must keep waste in the digester for at least 28 days.
Under exemption T24, the exemption holder must collect and burn the biogas produced by the AD process to produce energy.
Under exemption T24, the exemption holder must use a net rated thermal input of less than 0.4MW on the AD plant biogas burner.
Under exemption T24, where there is more than one burner associated with the AD plant, the combined net rated thermal input must be less than 0.4MW.
Anaerobic digestion involves decomposing waste in an atmosphere with no oxygen and requires a purpose-built plant.
Once the AD process is completed it produces a digestate.

Where the only waste feedstock to the anaerobic digestion process is farmyard manure or slurry, the resulting digestate may be spread on agricultural land as a fertiliser or soil conditioner without being regulated as waste.
An exemption holder spreading digestate derived solely from farmyard manure or slurry must still comply with the code of good agricultural practice.
An exemption holder spreading digestate derived solely from farmyard manure or slurry must comply with any requirements for nitrate vulnerable zones.
A person who meets the requirements of exemption T24 must register this exemption with the Environment Agency.
If an operator cannot comply with the limits and quantities in exemption T24, they must apply for an environmental permit.
Digestate is defined as the output from the treatment of organic and biodegradable waste by anaerobic digestion.

Net rated thermal input is defined as the rate at which fuel can be burned at the maximum continuous rating of the appliance, multiplied by the net calorific value of the fuel and expressed as megawatts thermal.
Plant tissue waste is defined as all materials of plant origin such as trees, shrubbery, branches with or without foliage, leaves or foliage wood and vegetation.
The Environment Agency updates its executive directors and board on corporate scorecard progress every 3 months.
The Environment Agency shares its corporate scorecard with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).
Corporate scorecard 2020 to 2021 quarter 4 starts 1 January 2021 and ends 31 March 2021.
A green rating on the Environment Agency scorecard means performance is at or above the target set.
An amber rating on the Environment Agency scorecard means performance is falling slightly short of the target.
A red rating on the Environment Agency scorecard means there are improvements to be made.
By Q4 2020 to 2021, the Environment Agency had cumulatively better protected 314,361 homes since April 2015, exceeding the target of 300,000.
The government invested £2.6 billion in flood and coastal defence schemes since 2015, with the Environment Agency and partners completing more than 850 projects to better protect over 300,000 homes.
The Humber: Hull Frontage project is a £42 million scheme that will better protect 11,344 homes in Hull from tidal surges.
At the end of the 2020 to 2021 financial year, 94.5% of flood and coastal risk management assets nationally were at required condition, against the published target of 98%.
For 2020 to 2021, the government provided £120 million of additional funding for flood and coastal risk management asset repairs.

Where flood and coastal risk management assets are below required condition, the Environment Agency will take action to ensure flood risk is effectively managed until the asset is fully repaired or replaced.
The Environment Agency did not meet its target to complete 90% of climate adaptation actions by the end of 5 years (April 2021).
The Environment Agency will publish its third climate adaptation report in summer 2021.
The Environment Agency estimates approximately 6,408 trained and capable incident staff are ready to respond to incidents, representing 107% of the 6,000 target.
Sulphur dioxide emissions per tonne of oil processed from refineries have reduced by more than 10%, meeting the Environment Agency's reduction target.
Nitrogen oxide emissions from the refineries sector have not reduced by 10%, failing to meet the Environment Agency's reduction target.
Revised limits in environmental permits during 2018 contributed to reductions in sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxide emissions below the 2017 baseline.

In 2020 to 2021 (April 2020 to March 2021) a total of 4,551 km of the water environment was enhanced, bringing the cumulative total to over 11,000 km since the updated 2015 river basin management plans, exceeding the overall target of 8,000 km by December 2021.
In 2020 to 2021, 88 km of rivers and groundwaters were protected from deterioration.
In 2020 to 2021, the Environment Agency and partners created 744 ha of new priority habitat and restored a further 1,153 ha.
Since 2010 to 2011, the Environment Agency has created a total of 8,353 ha of new priority habitat against a challenging ambition of 10,000 ha towards the aims of biodiversity 2020.
At year end 2020 to 2021, 97.8% of planning applications were determined in line with Environment Agency advice, against a target of 97%.
In Q4 2020 to 2021, high risk illegal waste sites were reduced by 12 compared to Q3.
In Q4 2020 to 2021, active illegal waste sites were reduced by 106 compared to Q3.
In Q4 2020 to 2021, 95 illegal waste sites were stopped and 80 new sites were identified, 21 of which were high risk.
By the end of 2020 to 2021, the Environment Agency achieved 74% of its carbon emission reduction target for the year.
The Environment Agency saw a 32% reduction (nearly 10,000 tonnes less) in overall direct carbon emissions in 2020 to 2021 compared to the previous year.
The Environment Agency's emissions from business travel decreased by 48% in 2020 to 2021 compared to the previous year.
Operational assets made up nearly 50% of the Environment Agency's direct carbon emissions in 2020 to 2021.
The Environment Agency invested £1.6 billion on the environment in 2020 to 2021, around 20% above the level of investment in 2019 to 2020.
In Q4 2020 to 2021, the proportion of BAME employees in the Environment Agency stood at 4.5%, against a target of 14% for 2020 to 2021.
The BAME workforce target of 14% will be reviewed in March 2022 when initial 2021 census data should be available.
In Q4 2020 to 2021, 44% (40) of the Environment Agency's executive managers were female, against a target of 50%.
The Lost Time Incident Frequency Rate (LTIFR) is defined as work-related injuries resulting in a day or more of lost time, measured per 100,000 hours worked.
The Environment Agency's LTIFR target is a very low ceiling of 0.11 injuries per 100,000 hours worked.
Applicants must read the Capital Grants 2026 guidance to understand the rules and how to apply for this item.
Applicants applying for this item as part of a Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier application must read the CSHT applicant's guide to understand the rules and how to apply.
Applicants can be paid up to £6,800 per hectare if they use this item to create new woodland.
The payment rate for native tree species restocking applies where over 80% of restocked trees are native.
The payment rate for non-native tree species restocking applies if over 20% of restocked trees are not native.
This item may be used with Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier and Woodland Tree Health grants to plant trees on land that has not been woodland for at least 10 years before the date of application.
This item may be used with Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier and Woodland Tree Health grants to plant trees on woodland following felling due to a plant health disease.
This item may be used with the approval of Catchment Sensitive Farming in Capital Grants to plant tree shelter belts.
This item must not be used if tree planting could damage areas of ecological, archaeological or historic interest.
This item must not be used to plant woodland for producing Christmas trees or biomass.
This item must not be used to plant cricket bat willows.
This item must not be used for restocking woodlands following a conditional felling licence, except where the licence has been issued for tree health reasons.
This item must not be used to plant ash trees.
When planting trees, the grant holder must use tree species that support the planting objectives and are appropriate for the soil and climatic conditions.
When planting trees, the grant holder must select planting stock that complies with the Forest Reproductive Material Regulations 2002 (where applicable).
When planting trees, the grant holder must select planting stock that is appropriate in size and type for the planting site.
When planting trees, the grant holder must create a suitable planting site by spraying herbicide in advance, scarifying in lines, or preparing a bare patch of land (screefing) with a spade.
When planting trees, the grant holder must use a planting method with roots fully inserted in the ground and the root collar at ground level, and the tree must be firmed in without damaging the stem.

When planting trees, the grant holder must protect young trees by attaching a 0.6m spiral guard secured with a cane, unless agreed otherwise with the Forestry Commission or Natural England adviser for broadleaved species.
When planting trees, the grant holder must plant trees when dormant and attach the spiral guard immediately if needed.
When planting trees, the grant holder must maintain spiral guards until the trees are established and remove the spiral and cane when the tree no longer needs support.
When planting trees, the grant holder must replace dead trees in the following planting season, and at the end of the agreement each grant-funded tree must be alive and in good condition.
When planting new woodland, the grant holder must prepare a habitat creation plan that includes the planting objectives.
When planting new woodland, the grant holder must prepare a habitat creation plan that includes the species to be planted.
When planting new woodland, the grant holder must prepare a habitat creation plan that includes the number of trees to be planted per hectare.
When planting new woodland, the grant holder must prepare a habitat creation plan that includes the percentage and location of open space and how it will be protected.
When planting new woodland, the grant holder must prepare a habitat creation plan that includes a map showing the areas where trees will be planted and open space.

The grant holder must prepare a tree planting plan designed to capture ammonia from livestock housing, slurry or digestate stores, livestock housing for pigs or poultry, and free-range areas for pigs or poultry.
The grant holder must plant trees within 20 metres of the livestock building, slurry or digestate stores, or the entire ranging area for free-range poultry or pigs.
The grant holder must obtain approval from Catchment Sensitive Farming for their application where planting trees to capture ammonia.
When planting, the grant holder must plant trees based on their design plan.
Before planting trees, the grant holder must check whether a forestry Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) is required.
Planting less than 0.5 hectares will never require an Environmental Impact Assessment as it does not meet the definition of afforestation.

Planting between 0.5 hectares and 2 hectares will typically mean an Environmental Impact Assessment application is not required, unless there is a statutory designation on the site such as a SSSI, or other environmental sensitivity such as an archaeological feature.
Planting more than 2 hectares will typically require an Environmental Impact Assessment application.
The grant holder must keep photographs of the completed work and provide them with their claim.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request any consents or permissions connected with the work.
The grant holder must keep and provide on request receipted invoices or bank statements where a receipted invoice is unavailable.
The Forestry Commission or the Animal and Plant Health Agency must confirm cases of ash dieback.
A statutory plant health notice (SPHN) must confirm Phytophthora ramorum, Phytophthora pluvialis or Sweet chestnut blight infection.
The grant holder must follow relevant laws and codes of practice if spraying herbicide.

The Improving Farm Productivity Grant may be used to fund capital items that improve farm and horticulture productivity through the use of robotic or autonomous equipment and systems to aid crop and livestock production.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant may be used to fund the installation of slurry acidification equipment for improved nutrient management and reduced ammonia emissions.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant must not be used to generate renewable energy.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant must not be used to buy irrigation equipment.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant must not be used to fund equipment used in the processing and packing of fruit or vegetables.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant must not be used to fund crop storage.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant must not be used to fund anaerobic digesters.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant prioritises projects that improve productivity.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant prioritises projects that improve the environment.
The Improving Farm Productivity Grant prioritises projects that introduce innovation.
Eligible projects must improve farm productivity through improving efficiency and effectiveness of primary agricultural or horticultural production.
Eligible projects must reduce the farm's environmental impact resulting in a positive contribution to net zero and greenhouse gas reduction.
Eligible projects must improve nutrient and resource management that supports optimisation of field and livestock operations.
Eligible projects must support the adoption of automation in areas where access to labour is an issue.
Some capital items are only available in Countryside Stewardship High and Medium Priority Areas for Water or Air Quality and with the support and approval of Catchment Sensitive Farming (CSF).
The capital items an applicant applies for must address the water or air pollution as affecting that catchment or area in order to receive CSF support and approval.
An applicant must clearly show how pollutants are currently reaching a watercourse or underground aquifer in order to receive CSF support and approval.
In the case of air quality, an applicant must identify where the ammonia emissions are coming from in order to receive CSF support and approval.
An applicant must show how the proposed work will prevent losses of water pollutants or ammonia emissions in order to receive CSF support and approval.
CSF support and approval will be targeted at farms where the largest improvements in water and air quality are likely to be achieved.
The impact of a water pollutant or ammonia on a sensitive protected site will be considered as part of targeting CSF support and approval.
An applicant must show that they have included a suitable range of capital items to prevent the pollutant reaching a watercourse or the atmosphere.
Where work is needed over a number of years, an applicant may need to submit a number of applications.
An applicant must agree with CSF how phased work is staged across applications.
An application that meets local water and air objectives will receive a higher score.
An applicant must have approval from CSF before applying for certain capital items listed in the CSF-approval table.
If an applicant applies for CSF-approval capital items without having CSF approval, those items will be removed from the application.
Mid Tier multi-year options and capital items each have an associated code, name, and payment rate.
Applicants may use the MAGIC website to find water and air quality information for their land.
Applicants may find the water and air quality objectives for their local area in their local Statement of Priorities.
If an application meets local water and air quality objectives, the applicant will receive a higher score.
Applicants may use the Countryside Stewardship Online Tool to help identify the types of options and capital items that can address water and air quality priorities in the local area.
Applicants may only include certain options and capital items in their agreement with the support and approval of Catchment Sensitive Farming (CSF).
To receive CSF support and approval, the options and capital items applied for must address the water or air pollutants identified as affecting that catchment or area.
Applicants must clearly show how water pollutants are currently reaching a watercourse or underground aquifer in order to receive CSF support and approval.
In the case of air quality, applicants must identify where ammonia emissions are coming from in order to receive CSF support and approval.
Applicants must show how the proposed work will prevent water or air pollutant losses in order to receive CSF support and approval.
CSF can only consider Countryside Stewardship applications and approve items where they improve water quality by reducing diffuse water pollution from agriculture.
CSF can only consider Countryside Stewardship applications and approve items where they reduce flood risk by using flood management measures.
CSF can only consider Countryside Stewardship applications and approve items where they improve air quality by reducing ammonia emissions.
CSF can only consider Countryside Stewardship applications and approve items where they address a priority pollutant in the catchment or area.
Where work is needed over several years, applicants must agree with CSF how that work is phased.
Applicants must have approval from CSF to apply for the options and capital items listed in the CSF-required table.
If an applicant applies for CSF-required options and capital items without CSF approval, those items will be removed from the application.
Applicants must check their local Statement of Priorities to confirm that the options and capital items they want address the issues in their area before applying for CSF-required items.

Applicants must complete the 'Request Support and Approval from Catchment Sensitive Farming' form, explaining how their application will improve water quality, improve air quality, reduce flood risk, or address a priority pollutant in the catchment or area.
Applicants must send the completed CSF request form to the relevant CSF e-mail address at least 10 weeks before they intend to submit their Countryside Stewardship application.
There is no guarantee that a CSF request for support and approval will be considered.
Applicants must read the Capital Grants 2026 guidance to understand the rules and how to apply for item RP14.
Applicants applying for item RP14 as part of a Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) application must read the CSHT applicant's guide to understand the rules and how to apply.
The payment rate for item RP14 (yard inspection pit) is £200 per unit.
Item RP14 improves existing outdoor yards, reducing the risk of runoff and water pollution.
Item RP14 can help protect, recover, and improve biodiversity on the applicant's land.
Item RP14 may only be used with support from Catchment Sensitive Farming in areas targeted for the reduction of water pollution from agriculture.
Item RP14 may only be used where the existing uncovered yards are made from concrete, hard core, tarmac, or bare earth.
Applicants must get advice from the Environment Agency to check if consents or permits are required before starting work on item RP14.
Approval may be required if the works under item RP14 are designed to transfer slurry or silage effluent.
Approval may be required if the works under item RP14 need to meet water resources control of water pollution regulations.
Item RP14 cannot be used on covered or indoor yards.
The inspection pit or catch pit built under item RP14 must have an internal area of at least 0.36 square metres.
The inspection pit or catch pit built under item RP14 must have a pit opening of at least 600 millimetres in diameter.
The inspection pit or catch pit built under item RP14 must be on a concrete base that is at least 250mm thick, with block walls rendered internally and externally.
Pit walls under item RP14 may be constructed with solid concrete blocks at least 200mm thick.
Pit walls under item RP14 may be constructed with engineering brickwork at least 200mm thick.
Pit walls under item RP14 may be constructed with proprietary precast reinforced concrete.
Pit walls under item RP14 may be constructed with a proprietary plastic or uPVC liner with a concrete surround at least 150mm thick.
All items under item RP14 must be installed according to the manufacturer's instructions to support the weight of traffic going over the pit.
The cover and frame of item RP14 must be designed to carry a load appropriate to its situation, and must be at least 10 kilo newtons per m2 in high traffic areas.
The cover and frame of item RP14 must be embedded securely so it is level with or below the surrounding surface.
If item RP14 has an open grating, it must be built at least 10 metres away from a slurry or silage store.
The inspection pit channel under item RP14 must be benched half round, equal in diameter to the largest inlet pipe, and extending the full length of the pit.
Side connections under item RP14 must discharge in the direction of flow of the main channel.
A pluggable catch pit and sump of at least 450mm deep with a submersible pump must be installed at the point where clean drainage water leaves the yard under item RP14.
Agreement holders must monitor the drainage water for contaminants under item RP14.
Agreement holders must keep written support from their Catchment Sensitive Farming adviser and provide this with their application for item RP14.

Agreement holders must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the site during different stages of construction, or contracts, invoices or other documents confirming the technical specification for the completed works.
Agreement holders must keep and provide with their claim photographs of the completed works under item RP14.
Agreement holders must keep and provide with their claim any consents or permissions connected with the work under item RP14.
Agreement holders must keep and provide on request receipted invoices or bank statements where a receipted invoice is unavailable for item RP14.
Agreement holders must keep and provide on request photographs of the site before works start under item RP14.
Manure is excrement or urine from any farmed animal apart from fish, and can include litter (pieces of animal bedding).
Guano is excrement from bats or wild sea birds.
Digestive tract content is any content from the digestive tract of a mammal or ratite (ostrich, emu or rhea).
Manure, guano or digestive tract content may be sold as fertiliser in the EU if the producer meets certain processing requirements.
If EU processing standards are not met, a product may still be sold as fertiliser if it is partially processed using heat or pressure.
A producer must contact APHA to apply for approval to make fertiliser from partially processed manure or guano.
A producer who receives a fertiliser made from manure, guano or digestive tract content in bulk and repackages it for final sale must obtain approval to store an organic fertiliser or soil improver.
A producer seeking approval to store an organic fertiliser or soil improver must contact APHA.

Non-poultry manure, guano or digestive tract content may be spread on the producer's own land without restrictions under ABP regulations, though an Environment Agency waste disposal permit may be required unless an exemption applies.
Non-poultry manure, guano or digestive tract content may be supplied for use on other farms or market gardens without restrictions under ABP regulations.
It is good practice to avoid spreading manure, guano or digestive tract content on land where farmed animals have access.
It is good practice to stop animals grazing on land for 21 days (or for 2 months in the case of pigs) after manure, guano or digestive tract content has been spread on it.
Further restrictions on spreading manure, guano or digestive tract content may be imposed during an outbreak of a notifiable disease.
A poultry keeper must inspect birds regularly and remove any dead ones from the litter immediately so carcasses do not end up in the manure.
A poultry keeper must not dispose of feathers or cracked eggs in manure.
A poultry keeper who spreads poultry manure on land where farmed animals graze should stop animals grazing on the land for 21 days (or 2 months for pigs).
A poultry keeper must stack manure as far as possible from livestock.
A poultry keeper must fence in stacked manure until it is used.
An operator must get approval to burn poultry manure in a combustion unit.
A farmer must complete and keep a self-assessment and annual vet review form when using recycled manure solids (RMS) as cattle bedding, to show the Red Tractor assessor and their vet.
Unprocessed manure cannot be imported from countries outside the EU.
Approval is not required to store manure, guano or digestive tract content at the farm or premises where it is produced.
Approval is not required to store manure, guano or digestive tract content at the place where it is going to be used, as long as it is stacked or kept in a slurry tank or tanker until used.
Approval is not required to store abattoir digestive tract content (normally mixed with lairage manure) at the abattoir, or to store lairage manure at the place where it is going to be used.
If manure, guano or digestive tract content is stored anywhere other than where it was produced or where it is going to be used, the storage site must be approved to store category 2 ABP material.
An operator must complete form ABPR3 and send it to APHA to get approved to store ABP material.

If digestive tract content or guano is moved off the premises where it was produced, the transporter must follow the requirements for transporting ABP material and keeping commercial documents with ABP material.
When handling manure, a person must follow transport and commercial document requirements, except when moving manure between farms or from a farm to the place where it is going to be used.
Manure, guano or digestive tract content does not need to be labelled as ABP material.

The UK has adopted legally-binding international targets to reduce emissions of fine particulate matter, ammonia, nitrogen oxides, sulphur dioxide, and non-methane volatile organic compounds by 2020 and 2030.
New legislation will create an England-wide power to control major sources of air pollution in line with the risk they pose to public health and the environment.
New legislation will create new local powers to take action in areas with an air pollution problem.
The UK has set stringent targets to cut emissions by 2020 and 2030, with the goal of reducing harm to human health from air pollution by half.
The government will invest £10 million in improving modelling, data and analytical tools to give a more precise picture of current air quality and the impact of future policies on it.
The government will increase transparency by bringing local and national monitoring data together into a single accessible portal for information on air quality monitoring and modelling.
Air quality is the largest environmental health risk in the UK.
The government will set a new, ambitious, long-term target to reduce people's exposure to PM2.5.
The government will publish evidence early in 2019 to examine what action would be needed to meet the WHO annual mean guideline limit of 10 μg/m3 for PM2.5.
By implementing the Clean Air Strategy 2019 policies, the number of people living in locations above the WHO guideline PM2.5 level of 10 μg/m3 will be reduced by 50% by 2025.
The government will provide a personal air quality messaging system to inform the public, particularly those vulnerable to air pollution, about the air quality forecast.
The government will work with the Medical Royal Colleges and the General Medical Council to embed air quality into health professions' education and training.
The government will commit to a new target for the reduction of damaging deposition of reactive forms of nitrogen.
The government will provide guidance for local authorities explaining how cumulative impacts of nitrogen deposition on natural habitats should be mitigated and assessed through the planning system.

All new Renewable Heat Incentive applicants must submit relevant permits and exemptions to evidence their compliance with all local and national environmental regulations, including air quality impacts.
The government will consult on making coal to biomass conversions ineligible for future allocation rounds of the contracts for difference scheme.
The government has committed more than £3.5 billion to tackle poor air quality through cleaner road transport.
The government has set plans to end the sale of new conventional petrol and diesel cars and vans by 2040.
New legislation will enable the Transport Secretary to compel manufacturers to recall vehicles and non-road mobile machinery for any failures in their emissions control system.
New legislation will enable the Transport Secretary to take effective action against tampering with vehicle emissions control systems.
By spring 2019, the rail industry will produce recommendations and a route map to phase out diesel-only trains by 2040.
By spring 2019, the government will publish guidelines to advise ports on how to develop effective and targeted Air Quality Strategies.
Following publication of port air quality guidelines, ports within scope will be required to produce Air Quality Strategies by the end of 2019.
Burning wood and coal in open fires and stoves makes up 38% of the UK's primary emissions of fine particulate matter (PM2.5).
The government will legislate to prohibit the sale of the most polluting fuels.
Only the cleanest stoves will be available for sale by 2022.
The government will make changes to existing smoke control legislation to make it easier to enforce.
The government will consult on changes to Building Regulations standards for ventilation in homes and other buildings, to help reduce the harmful build-up of indoor air pollutants.
The agriculture sector accounts for 88% of UK emissions of ammonia.
The government will regulate to reduce ammonia emissions from farming by requiring adoption of low emissions farming techniques.
The government will extend environmental permitting to the dairy and intensive beef sectors.
The government will regulate to minimise pollution from fertiliser use.
The government will consider closing the regulatory gap between the current Ecodesign and medium combustion plant regulations to tackle emissions from plants in the 500kW to 1MW thermal input range.
The government will bring forward provisions on air quality in 2019, including an up-to-date legislative framework for tackling air pollution at national and local level.
Neighbouring local authorities and other public bodies must work collectively to tackle air pollution.

The UK government will work in partnership with the governments of Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland to develop a National Air Pollution Control Programme as required under the National Emissions Ceilings Directive for publication in 2019.
The Rural Payments Agency (RPA) is allowed to process personal data when it needs to carry out a public task, under article 6(1)(e) of the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR).
The relevant law governing RPA's processing of personal data for socio-economic measures is the Environmental Stewardship (England) Regulations 2005.
RPA has a legal obligation to provide beneficiary data to Defra, who publish it to meet EU retained legislation aimed at promoting transparency.
If a person has included personal data about other people in their records, they must tell those other people.
A person who has included personal data about others in their records must provide those other people with a copy of the RPA socio-economic measures privacy notice.
RPA stores, transfers and processes personal data on servers in the United Kingdom (UK) and the European Union (EU).
For the Small Grants scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Improving Forestry Productivity Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Large Grants scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Cumbrian Countryside Access Fund Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the European Innovation Partnership Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Growth Programme - Business Development scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Growth Programme - Food Processing scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Leader Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 5 years.
For the Adding Value to Agri-Food Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 7 years.
For the Growth Programme - Rural Broadband Infrastructure scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 7 years.
For the Growth Programme - Rural Tourism Infrastructure scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 7 years.
For the Improving Farm Productivity Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 7 years.
For the Farming Recovery Fund, RPA keeps personal information for 7 years.
For the Water Resource Management Scheme (listed under 7-year retention), RPA keeps personal information for 7 years.

For the Adding Value Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.

For the Slurry Infrastructure Scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.

For the Farming Equipment & Technology Fund, RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.

For the Smaller Abattoir Fund, RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.

For the Laying Hen Housing for Health and Welfare scheme, RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.

For the Water Resource Management Scheme (Grant Funding Agreement context), RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.

For the Water Restoration Fund, RPA keeps personal information for 3 years for data submitted through the application eligibility checker or full application, and for 7 years from the end of the agreement where a Grant Funding Agreement is entered into.
RPA may process a data subject's personal data including their email address in any questions or feedback they send to RPA.
RPA may use a data subject's personal information to contact them about future grants and updates to farming policy that would be relevant to them.
RPA may potentially use a data subject's personal information in media campaigns to promote outcomes, and must contact them to request their agreement before doing so.

RPA shares personal data with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra), Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA), Environment Agency, Natural England, Forestry Commission, Department for Business and Trade, Scottish Government Rural Payments and Inspections Directorate, Welsh Government Rural Payments Wales, Broadband UK, the European Union, and the European Structural and Investment Fund Committee.

Socio-economic measures consist of the following services: Adding Value to Agri-Food Scheme, Small Grants, Improving Farm Productivity Scheme, Improving Forestry Productivity Scheme, Large Grants, Cumbrian Countryside Access Fund Scheme, European Innovation Partnership Scheme, Farming Recovery Fund, Growth Programme - Business Development, Growth Programme - Food Processing, Growth Programme - Rural Broadband Infrastructure, Growth Programme - Rural Tourism Infrastructure, and Leader Scheme.

The following services include Grant Funding Agreements: Adding Value Scheme, Slurry Infrastructure Scheme, Farming Equipment and Technology Fund, Smaller Abattoir Fund, Laying Hen Housing for Health and Welfare, Water Resource Management Scheme, and Water Restoration Fund.
An environmental permit for a waste operation, or a relevant exemption, is required when storing zoo manure (waste code 20 01 99) to benefit land.
An environmental permit for a waste operation is required when spreading zoo manure (waste code 20 01 99) to benefit land.
The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement provided the activity meets the description set out in RPS 313.
The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement provided the operator complies with the conditions set out in RPS 313.
The activity must not cause (or be likely to cause) pollution of the environment or harm to human health.
The activity must not cause a risk to water, air, soil, plants or animals.
The activity must not cause a nuisance through noise or odours.
The activity must not adversely affect the countryside or places of special interest.
RPS 313 applies to storing and spreading manure and slurry (including soiled bedding made from plant tissue only) from herbivorous zoo animals to benefit land.
The operator must store zoo manure in a secure place where it will be spread.
The operator must only spread zoo manure from herbivorous animals on agricultural land.
The operator must only store up to 200 tonnes of waste zoo manure at any one time.
The operator must only spread up to 50 tonnes of zoo manure per hectare over any 12-month period.
The operator must keep records for 2 years from the date of the last use of RPS 313 to show compliance with the RPS.
The operator must make compliance records available to the Environment Agency on request.
The operator must not spread more manure than is needed to benefit the land.
The operator must not spread zoo manure on land that has been frozen for 12 hours or more in the preceding 24 hours.
The operator must not spread zoo manure on land that is waterlogged, frozen or covered in snow.
The operator must not store zoo manure for more than 12 months before spreading.
RPS 313 relates to an activity that the Environment Agency considers is potentially suitable to be an exemption under the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016.
The storage of zoo manure at the zoo is covered by the non-waste framework directive exemption: Waste exemption NWFD 2 temporary storage at the place of production.
The Environment Agency intends to review RPS 313 by 1 January 2028.
The Environment Agency may withdraw or amend RPS 313 before the review date if it considers it necessary, including where the activity that RPS 313 relates to has not changed.
RPS 313 remains in force until it is removed from GOV.UK or is otherwise identified as having been withdrawn.
If an operator can no longer comply with RPS 313, they must stop the activity to which RPS 313 relates.
If an operator can no longer comply with RPS 313, they must immediately tell the Environment Agency by emailing enquiries@environment-agency.gov.uk with 'RPS 313' in the subject line.
Storing and treating litter, straw, slurry and washwater from an animal disease outbreak requires an environmental permit for a waste operation.

The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement provided the activity and circumstances meet the description set out in RPS 36.
The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement provided the operator complies with the conditions set out in RPS 36.
The activity must not cause, or be likely to cause, pollution of the environment or harm to human health.
The activity must not cause a risk to water, air, soil, plants or animals.
The activity must not cause a nuisance through noise or odours.
The activity must not adversely affect the countryside or places of special interest.
RPS 36 applies only to waste (litter, straw, slurry, and washwater) generated during cleansing and disinfection after an animal disease outbreak has been confirmed by APHA.

Slurry is defined as liquid or semi-liquid matter with a consistency enabling it to be pumped or discharged by gravity, composed of excreta produced by livestock while in a yard or building, or a mixture of livestock excreta, livestock bedding, rainwater and washings from a yard or building used by livestock.
RPS 36 applies only when an animal disease outbreak has been confirmed by APHA.
Operators must follow the APHA requirements when storing and treating waste from an animal disease outbreak.
Operators must inform their local Environment Agency office before treating litter, straw, slurry and washwater waste from an animal disease outbreak.
Operators must keep records for 2 years (3 years for hazardous waste) to show compliance with RPS 36.
Operators must make their RPS 36 compliance records available to the Environment Agency on request.
Operators may release washwater to the foul sewer if they have written approval from their sewerage undertaker.
Operators may use a registered waste carrier to transport waste to an appropriately permitted facility using waste code 02 01 06 for washwater containing litter, straw, manure and slurry.
Operators may use a registered waste carrier to transport washwater to an appropriately permitted facility using waste code 16 10 02 or 16 10 01.
For spreading washwater to land, operators must complete a disposal of disinfectant washwater form and email it to PSC-WaterQuality@environment-agency.gov.uk.
For yard storage, operators must store waste litter, straw, slurry and washwater on an impermeable surface.

For yard storage, operators must use a collection and containment system for liquid runoff that meets the requirements of the Water Resources (Control of Pollution) (Silage, Slurry and Agricultural Fuel Oil) (England) Regulations 2010.
For field storage, operators must only store waste litter and straw (not slurry or washwater).
For field storage, operators must store waste litter and straw in packed heaps with an A-shaped profile.
For field storage, operators must not store waste litter and straw heaps for more than 12 months.
For field storage, operators must not store waste litter and straw heaps within 10 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters.
For field storage, operators must not store waste litter and straw heaps within 50 metres of a spring, well or borehole.
For field storage, operators must not store waste litter and straw heaps where there is a significant risk of pollution entering inland freshwaters or coastal waters.
For field storage, operators must not store waste litter and straw heaps in areas at risk of flooding.
Routine cleansing and disinfection does not require an environmental permit.
RPS 36 relates to an activity that the Environment Agency considers is potentially suitable to be an exemption under the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016.
The Environment Agency will review RPS 36 by 1 October 2026.
The Environment Agency may withdraw or amend RPS 36 before the review date if it considers it necessary, including where the activity and circumstances have not changed.
RPS 36 remains in force until it is removed from GOV.UK or is otherwise identified as having been withdrawn.
If an operator can no longer comply with RPS 36, they must stop the activity to which RPS 36 relates.
If an operator can no longer comply with RPS 36, they must tell the Environment Agency immediately.
An operator must have an environmental permit for a waste operation when storing and treating litter, straw, slurry and washwater from an animal disease outbreak.
An operator must comply with the conditions of an environmental permit when storing and treating litter, straw, slurry and washwater from an animal disease outbreak.

The Environment Agency will not normally take enforcement action against an operator who does not comply with the environmental permit requirement, provided the operator meets the requirements in RPS 36.
RPS 36 does not apply to any legal requirements other than the environmental permit requirement for storing and treating litter, straw, slurry and washwater from an animal disease outbreak.
Ammonia emission factors must be used for carrying out ammonia assessments as part of an intensive farming permit application.
Ammonia emission factors must be used for calculating annual emissions for reporting to the pollution inventory.
For typical free range poultry systems, birds are estimated to spend 10% of a 24-hour period on the range.
For typical free range poultry systems, birds are estimated to spend 90% of a 24-hour period within the housing.
Operators should use the combined housing and ranging emission factors for pollution inventory reporting.
If a housing system is not listed in this guide, an operator may need to provide evidence of a site-specific ammonia emission factor as part of their permit application.
Standard emission factors may be adjusted where a lower crude protein content is used in the animal's diet.
Standard emission factors may be adjusted where other ammonia mitigation measures are used.
Standard emission factors may be adjusted where an occupancy rate significantly lower than the standard rate is used.
The ammonia emission factor figures are based on best available information.
Operators may obtain a copy of the report that informs the emission factors guidance from their Environment Agency permitting officer or site officer.

An intensive farming permit applicant must follow the risk assessment guide when applying for a permit or to change (vary) a permit for intensive farming under the Environmental Permitting Regulations.
As part of the risk assessment, an intensive farming permit applicant must assess ammonia emissions.
As part of the risk assessment, an intensive farming permit applicant must assess odour emissions.
As part of the risk assessment, an intensive farming permit applicant must assess noise emissions.
As part of the risk assessment, an intensive farming permit applicant must assess dust and bioaerosol emissions.
An intensive farming permit applicant may request pre-application advice to arrange a pre-application discussion for their intensive farming permit.
An intensive farming permit applicant must have a completed ammonia screening assessment before applying for a new intensive farming permit.
An intensive farming permit applicant must have a completed ammonia screening assessment before applying to change (vary) an existing permit that has the potential to affect ammonia emissions.

If an intensive farming operator uses a housing system not listed in the standard ammonia emission factors, they may need to provide evidence of a site-specific ammonia emission factor as part of their permit application.
An intensive farming permit applicant must have agreement from the Environment Agency before using any bespoke emission factors.
The Environment Agency will use standard emission factors for ammonia screening unless they have previously agreed otherwise.
The Environment Agency assesses ammonia emissions against three standards: ammonia critical level, nutrient nitrogen critical load, and acidity critical load.
An intensive farming permit applicant will need to submit detailed ammonia modelling if their proposed activities are within 250 metres of any protected conservation site.

An intensive farming permit applicant will need to submit detailed ammonia modelling if the ammonia screening assessment shows ammonia emissions from the proposal could affect protected conservation sites.

An intensive farming permit applicant will need to submit detailed ammonia modelling if the original permit for their farm included an 'improvement condition' requiring reduction of ammonia emissions at a protected nature conservation site.
An intensive farming permit applicant must do detailed ammonia modelling if their ammonia screening result exceeds 1% for SPAs, SACs and Ramsar sites.
An intensive farming permit applicant must do detailed ammonia modelling if their ammonia screening result exceeds 50% for SSSIs.
An intensive farming permit applicant must do detailed ammonia modelling if their ammonia screening result exceeds 100% for local nature sites.

If a farm is within 5 kilometres of an SSSI and its ammonia screening result is between the lower and upper screening thresholds, the Environment Agency will check other permitted farms in the area, and if combined emissions are above the upper threshold, the applicant will need to do detailed modelling.
The Environment Agency revised the standard ammonia emission factors in November 2024.
An intensive farming permit applicant must submit written odour and noise management plans as part of their application if there are receptors within 400 metres of the site boundary.
An intensive farming permit applicant must submit written odour and noise management plans as part of their application if the site has been the cause of odour or noise complaints in the past.
An intensive farming operator must submit a risk assessment of the impact of ammonia emissions from their site when applying for a new bespoke permit.

An intensive farming operator must submit a risk assessment of the impact of ammonia emissions from their site when applying to change (vary) an existing environmental permit in a way that will cause an increase of ammonia emissions to air from their farm.
An intensive farming operator generally cannot get a permit for activities that result in releasing ammonia into water.
An intensive farming operator must contact the Environment Agency before starting their application if they expect to release ammonia to water.
An intensive farming operator must assess the environmental impact of nitrogen dioxide and particulate emissions if they add a biomass boiler to an existing permitted site.
An intensive farming operator must assess the environmental impact of nitrogen dioxide and particulate emissions if they propose a biomass boiler in plans for a new site.

An intensive farming operator does not need to do an assessment of air emissions from a biomass boiler if the fuel comes from virgin timber, clean non-virgin timber, straw or miscanthus, and the appliance and installation comply with standards equivalent to the now closed Renewable Heat Incentive scheme, and the aggregate boiler net rated thermal input is less than 0.5MWth.

For a biomass boiler installation to be exempt from an air emissions assessment (where aggregate net rated thermal input is between 0.5MWth and 4MWth), the aggregate boiler net rated thermal input must be less than or equal to 4MWth.

For a biomass boiler installation to be exempt from an air emissions assessment (where aggregate net rated thermal input is between 0.5MWth and 4MWth), no individual boiler must have a net thermal input greater than 1MWth.

For a biomass boiler installation to be exempt from an air emissions assessment (where aggregate net rated thermal input is between 0.5MWth and 4MWth), the stack height must be at least 5 metres above the ground and must extend above the roof level of any building within 25 metres by at least 1 metre.

For a biomass boiler installation to be exempt from an air emissions assessment (where aggregate net rated thermal input is between 0.5MWth and 4MWth), there must be no receptors within 50 metres of the emission points.

For pig farms, a biomass boiler installation does not require an air emissions assessment if the aggregate boiler net rated thermal input is less than 1MWth and the stack height is greater than 1 metre above the roof level of buildings within 25 metres.
For pig farms, where there are no buildings within 25 metres, the biomass boiler stack height must be a minimum of 3 metres above ground to be exempt from an air emissions assessment.
For pig farms, a biomass boiler installation does not require an air emissions assessment if there are no SPAs, SACs, Ramsar sites or SSSIs within 500 metres of the emission points.

For pig farms, a biomass boiler installation does not require an air emissions assessment if there are no National Nature Reserves, Local Nature Reserves, Local Wildlife Sites or Ancient Woodlands within 100 metres of the emission points.

For pig farms where the aggregate boiler net rated thermal input is greater than 1MWth but less than 2MWth, no air emissions assessment is required if, in addition to meeting all other criteria, there are no receptors within 150 metres of the emission points.
Net rated thermal input means the rate at which fuel can be burned at the maximum continuous rating of the appliance, multiplied by the net calorific value of the fuel, expressed in MWth.
An intensive farming permit applicant must produce and submit a dust and bioaerosol management plan with their application if there are relevant receptors within 100 metres of their farm.
In a dust and bioaerosol management plan, the applicant must give details of control measures they will use to manage the risks from dust and bioaerosols from their farm.
An intensive farming permit applicant must submit their application within 6 months of their ammonia screening assessment to ensure the data remains current.
If an intensive farming permit application requires detailed modelling, the applicant must show that their emissions are at the acceptable levels in the screening thresholds table.
If an intensive farming permit application requires detailed modelling and emissions are not at acceptable levels, the applicant must provide plans to show how they will reduce them.

For SPAs, SACs, Ramsar sites and SSSIs, the Environment Agency may consult with Natural England before deciding whether to approve a permit where an applicant is unable to propose ways to reduce emissions below screening threshold levels.
A full applicant must use the same project in their full application as was used in their online application.
The main details of a project must not be significantly different in the full application compared to the project details entered in the online application.
A full applicant must provide a breakdown of project costs in their full application.
A full applicant must provide supplier details in their full application.
A full applicant must provide key dates and project milestones in their full application.
A full applicant must provide proposed project outputs in their full application.
A full applicant must provide a financial impact forecast in their full application.
A full applicant must provide an explanation of how the project will be funded in their full application.
A full applicant must show how the project will make their business more efficient and improve its productivity.
A full applicant must provide details of previous similar grants in their full application.
A full applicant must provide details of their agricultural inputs and outputs and how these might change as a result of the project.
A full applicant must provide a copy of the relevant planning application documents, including plans of the proposed development, that have been submitted to the Local Planning Authority (LPA).

A full applicant must provide written confirmation from the LPA that the planning submission has been received and is complete, and this document must include the planning reference number allocated to the application.
A full applicant must provide an indication from the LPA of the expected timescale for a planning decision.

A full applicant may submit a full application without planning permission having been received, providing a planning application has been made to the LPA and the required planning information is provided.
It will not be possible to offer grant funding until planning permission has been received.
Where planning consent for a project has not been obtained before submitting an application, the applicant must submit a copy of the planning permission to RPA by 31 December 2022.
If the LPA is unable to decide a planning application before 31 December 2022, the grant application will be considered ineligible and withdrawn from the scheme.
A full applicant must provide financial accounts for the most recent 3 years of trading as supporting information.
A new business or self-employed applicant must provide the latest tax returns, management accounts, or a statement of income and expenses from an accountant as supporting information.
A full applicant must provide financial and production projections as supporting information.
A full applicant must provide relevant permissions and consents, including planning consent, as supporting information.
A full applicant must provide 3 references to catalogue listings or formal tenders for each item with a value of £5,000 or more.
A full applicant must provide one quote for any items with a value up to £4,999.99.
No quotes are required for additional FETF slurry application equipment.
A full applicant must provide evidence of project funding as supporting information.
Where a full applicant purchases second-hand items, they must provide confirmation from the supplier that the item has a 5-year life and has not received previous grant funding.
A full applicant must provide evidence, in the form of a letter from a Chartered accountant or HMRC, that they are able to include irrecoverable VAT in their project costs, where applicable.
Where a joint venture is proposed, a full applicant must provide details and a draft agreement between parties.
A full applicant must provide letters of support from any third parties as supporting information.
Full applications will be scored against the Improving Farm Productivity grant scheme priorities, which are to improve productivity, improve the environment, and introduce innovation.
Full applications will be scored on how well the project costs represent value for money, including the amount of grant required to deliver the outcomes and outputs.
RPA will assess the current financial viability of the applicant's business as part of the full application assessment.
RPA will assess how the project may impact on the applicant's existing business operations.
RPA will assess how the applicant will fund the project until the grant is claimed.
RPA will assess how the applicant's business will benefit from the project financially.
RPA will assess how the project will be delivered within budget and on time.
RPA will assess that risks to project delivery have been identified and how they will be mitigated.
Midland Pig Producers Limited applied to the Environment Agency for an environmental permit for a pig unit and anaerobic digestion plant at Uttoxeter Road, DE65 5DL on 29 March 2011.

Permit application EPR/LP3930FA/A001 related to the installation of 14 pig houses with capacity for up to 24,500 pigs (2,500 sows, 14,000 production pigs, and up to 8,000 associated pigs from weaning).
Permit application EPR/LP3930FA/A001 included an anaerobic digestion plant and biogas combustion for the treatment of pig manures and slurries.
The permit application EPR/LP3930FA/A001 was subject to public consultation from 3 July 2013 to 31 July 2013.
In February 2015, the Environment Agency refused permit application EPR/LP3930FA/A001 on the grounds of odour.
In February 2015, the Environment Agency refused the application in accordance with the Environmental Permitting Regulations (England and Wales) 2010.
In August 2016, Midland Pig Producers Limited submitted an appeal to the Planning Inspector against the Environment Agency's decision to refuse the environmental permit.
On 7 August 2015, Midland Pig Producers Limited submitted an Appeal Form along with the Grounds of Appeal to the Secretary of State against the refusal of permit EPR/LP3930FA/A001.

Waste code 16 03 covers off-specification batches and unused products.
A waste holder may use waste code 16 03 for landspreading if they hold SR2010 No 4 (see table 2.2B).
For waste code 16 03, the waste holder must follow the information for 02 07 04 Materials unsuitable for consumption or processing.
Waste code 16 10 covers aqueous liquid wastes destined for off-site treatment.
A waste holder may use waste code 16 10 for landspreading if they hold SR2010 No 4 (see table 2.2B).
Waste code 16 10 is controlled by the animal by-products regulations.
For waste code 16 10, the waste holder must follow the guidance for the animal by-product industry.

Waste code 16 10 is the untreated wash waters from intermediate animal by-product plants, which are short-term storage facilities where animal by-products are bulked up and stored before being taken to a rendering plant handling categories 1, 2 and 3.

Wash waters under waste code 16 10 have essentially the same composition as untreated wash waters from rendering plants and poultry preparation plants, containing small amounts of blood and gut contents.

For waste code 16 10, the waste holder must also follow the information given for 02 02 99 Wash waters from animal by-product handling and processing plants that meet the waste water treatment requirements in the animal by-products regulations.
Waste code 16 10 may be used to provide agricultural benefit, including to supply nutrients.
Waste code 16 10 may be used to provide agricultural benefit, including to improve the water content of the soil.
Waste code 16 10 can cause odour during delivery, handling, storage and landspreading.
Waste code 16 10 can attract scavengers.
Waste code 16 10 can generate leachate.
Waste code 16 10 can result in flies and dust nuisance.
Waste code 16 10 can lead to adverse effects on soil micro-organism ecology.
For waste code 16 10, the waste holder must follow the guidance in 'Landspreading: how to manage soil health' for iodine.
For waste code 16 10, the waste holder must follow the guidance in 'Landspreading: how to manage soil health' to manage oil and fat trap wastes, as these wastes can be similar to oily wastes.
For waste code 16 10, subsurface injection to a depth of at least 15cm must be used where conditions allow.
For waste code 16 10, where shallower injection or surface application is used, the waste holder must work the wash waters into the land as soon as is practicable.
For waste code 16 10, the waste holder must work wash waters into the land before allowing grazing animals onto the land.
Waste exemption U15 is in charging band 2.
Under exemption U15, a farm operator may mix manure or slurry with ash from the incineration of pig and poultry carcasses.
Under exemption U15, a farm operator may spread the manure or slurry ash mix on their farm to benefit the soil.
Under exemption U15, a farm operator may store ash before mixing it with manure or slurry, and may store the manure or slurry ash mix before spreading it.
Under exemption U15, a farm operator must not take ash from neighbouring farms and mix it with manure and slurry on their farm.
Under exemption U15, a farm operator must not mix any ashes other than pig and poultry incineration ash with manure and slurry and spread the mix on their land.
Under exemption U15, a farm operator must not plough ash straight into the soil.
Waste used under exemption U15 must fit within the relevant waste code and description listed in the List of Wastes (LoW) Regulations.
The maximum amount of ash that may be spread under exemption U15 is 150 kg per hectare in any 12-month period.
A farm operator should only spread the amount of ash that the soil needs.
If more ash is applied than appears reasonable, the Environment Agency may consider this as disposal of waste.
Under exemption U15, ash or the ash mix must not be stored or spread within 10 metres of a watercourse.
Under exemption U15, ash or the ash mix must not be stored or spread within 50 metres of a spring, well or borehole.
Under exemption U15, the ash must be mixed with an equal or larger amount of manure or slurry before spreading it.
Under exemption U15, the ash must be stored in a secure place before it is spread.
Under exemption U15, ash must not be spread on land that has been frozen for 12 hours or more in the preceding 24 hours.
Under exemption U15, ash must not be spread on land that is frozen.
Under exemption U15, ash must not be spread on land that is waterlogged.
Under exemption U15, ash must not be spread on land that is covered by snow.
Pig and poultry carcasses must be incinerated in compliance with the Animal By-Product Regulations.
Exemption U15 can only take place on the same farm where the pigs or poultry died and were incinerated.
A farm operator must register a U15 exemption with the Environment Agency if they meet the requirements.
