The Environment Agency corporate scorecard Q4 2023 to 2024 covers the period starting 1 January 2024 and ending 31 March 2024.
During Q4 2023 to 2024, 16,709 properties were better protected from flooding and coastal erosion by 90 schemes.
The total properties better protected from flooding and coastal erosion for 2023 to 2024 was 28,971, against a target of 28,000.
The cumulative total of properties better protected from flooding since April 2021 is 88,272, exceeding the target of 87,351.
Environment Agency owned and maintained high consequence asset condition for Q4 2023 to 2024 is 92.6% at target condition, a reduction of 0.7% from 93.3% at the end of Q3.
Asset condition falling below required condition does not mean in every case that assets have structurally failed or that performance in a flood is compromised.
With more assets falling below target conditions than prior to the storms, there is an increased risk of asset failure and flood risk that will endure throughout 2024 and possibly beyond.
For climate impact plan actions set in 2023 to 2024, 75 out of 96 (78%) are on track or complete.
Of 71 adaptation actions, 53 (75%) are on track; of 25 net zero actions, 22 (88%) are on track for 2023 to 2024.
The Environment Agency has been announced as scheme administrator for the Clean Heat Market Mechanism, now planned to launch on 1 April 2025.
Staff utilisation for key incident response activities in Q4 2023 to 2024 was 113% of the total number needed.
A new incident response metric approach will be adopted in 2024 to 2025 to provide a more holistic view of resilience.

The air quality measure for 2023 to 2024 reports emissions of sulphur oxides (SOx), nitrogen oxides (NOx), fine particulate matter (PM2.5), and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) from four main industry sectors.
For 2023 to 2024, decreases in emissions were reported for all but one of the four monitored pollutants, with PM2.5 being the exception that increased.
In Q4 2023 to 2024, 1,302 km of water environment enhancements were recorded, with 1,257 km from the Countryside Stewardship Programme.
For 2023 to 2024, the Environment Agency reported 2,520 km of water environment enhanced, achieving 118% of its target to enhance 2,130 km across the year.

Since the Environment Agency began reporting the km enhanced corporate scorecard measure, 17,790 km have been reported as enhanced and 4,660 km as protected in rivers, lakes, coastal and groundwaters.
In 2023 to 2024, Environment Agency area teams created 385 ha of wildlife-rich habitat and restored another 1,527 ha.
The habitats created or restored will store 2,651 tonnes of CO2e per year, worth £14 million over 40 years at present value.
A site is considered compliant if assessed within the top 3 compliance bands (A-C), in line with published Environment Agency guidance on assessing and scoring permit compliance.
Of waste and installations permitted sites assessed in 2023, 97% were compliant with environmental permit requirements.
The number of serious environmental incidents from permitted sources is steady at between 125 and 160 incidents per year.
The high number of serious pollution incidents from the water sector is mainly due to the performance of Anglian Water, Southern Water, and Thames Water.
Water company performance means the sector cannot meet the requirements set out in the water industry strategic environmental requirements (WISER) guidance.
WISER guidance is designed to help water companies understand their environmental obligations and the regulators' expectations of them.
There were 47 serious incidents (verified data) at permitted waste sites at the end of calendar year 2023.
In the 12 months to end March 2024, there were 589 (73%) serious environmental incidents from an unpermitted or unknown source and 215 (27%) from permitted sites and activities.
Environment Agency planning performance in Q4 2023 to 2024 was 97%, and 97.4% for the whole year, against a green performance threshold.

In Q4 2023 to 2024, there were 29 planning decisions not in line with Environment Agency advice: 22 where the Environment Agency had raised objection and 7 where a requested condition was not included.
The number of high-risk illegal waste sites at the end of Q4 2023 to 2024 is 141, which is 10 fewer than the ceiling target.
In 2023 to 2024, the Environment Agency stopped 95 high-risk illegal waste sites identified as posing the greatest risk of harm to the environment and communities.
At year end 2023 to 2024, the Environment Agency's reported carbon emissions are within its carbon target, at 95% of the annual target.
The Environment Agency's year end carbon budget target for 2023 to 2024 is 282,916 tonnes of CO2e, an amended figure from the original target of 295,820 tonnes.
Only 19% of 92 planned net zero roadmap actions are on track to report a green status at year end 2023 to 2024.
Hire car usage has increased by 250% since the baseline year of 2019 to 2020, following the Environment Agency's decision to reduce the number of lease cars by 761 vehicles (21%) since 2019 to 2020.
Casual cars emit significantly more carbon than lease cars at 165g per km versus 37g per km; hire cars emit 121g per km.

The indicative outturn for 2023 to 2024 shows a total year end overspend of £54 million against budget allocation (2.8%), comprising £27 million overspend on resource budget (2.2%) and £27 million overspend on capital budget (3.9%).

Against the Defra group agreed departmental expenditure limits (DEL), the Environment Agency achieved a £3 million (0.3%) underspend on resource and a £3 million (0.4%) underspend on capital for 2023 to 2024.
The Environment Agency's current diversity target is that 18% of all employees are from black, Asian and minority ethnic backgrounds.
In Q4 2023 to 2024, 6.1% of Environment Agency employees were from minority ethnic backgrounds, an increase of 1.7% since Q1 2020 to 2021.
The Environment Agency has a target of 50% female representation at executive manager level; in Q4 2023 to 2024 the percentage of female executive managers was 45.7% (53 individuals).
Internal Environment Agency guidelines require that all roles recruited to grade 7 and above are conducted by diverse shortlisting and interview panels.
The Environment Agency lost time incident (LTI) frequency rate continues to be within the target range.
Applicants must complete the application appendix spreadsheet in full and send it as an Excel attachment with their application.

Applicants must fill in each tab of the application appendix spreadsheet in the order shown: Productivity details, A - Financial impact, B - Costs & suppliers, C - Funding & claims, D - Project milestones, E - Outputs.
The information entered in the 'Productivity details' tab will be used to score and assess how well the project meets the priorities for the Improving Farm Productivity theme.
Applicants must enter their business name, project name, and the project reference number from the invitation to submit a full application email.
If an applicant selects 'other autonomous robotic technology' at question 1, they must detail the make and model in the box provided at question 2.
Robotic equipment utilising an electric or renewable energy source will be prioritised over fossil fuel powered equipment.
Biofuels are considered as renewable energy under the Improving Farm Productivity grant scheme.
If there is no alternative to fossil fuel powered equipment, applicants must explain this at question 8.1.3 in the full application form.
If an applicant wishes to apply for more than three different items, they must email FTF@rpa.gov.uk to request a revised form.

Applicants must confirm the number of livestock places the project will accommodate (not the number of animals reared per year) when applying for advanced ventilation control systems or slurry mild acidification equipment.
Applicants must provide existing and future livestock place numbers in the table provided, even if there are no changes proposed.
By submitting the application appendix spreadsheet, the applicant is agreeing to the declaration set out in the 'Productivity details' tab.
Applicants must record, in Tab A, the additional costs or savings resulting from the project, entering the total income or cost in £ for 2 years after project completion.
Applicants must provide the supplier's name, quoted amount (excluding and including VAT), and the supplier's VAT and Companies House registration number (where applicable) for each item in Tab B.
Applicants must show all costs being included in their grant application in Tab B.
Applicants must also fill in the table for non-grant funded project expenditure showing costs critical to the project that are not being claimed as grant support.
Applicants must confirm in Tab C whether they are including irrecoverable VAT in their project costs by entering Y or N into the box on line 4.
Applicants must enter the grant rate as a number without a % sign in Tab C to calculate the grant amount, for example entering 40 for a 40% grant rate.
Grant is not normally paid on a deposit until the item is fully installed and operational.
Where a deposit payment is claimed, it must not be more than 40% of the value of the item.
Where a deposit payment is claimed, it must be clearly set out in the claim schedule.
If an applicant has more than one claim, the final claim must be at least 15% of the total grant amount being requested.
Applicants may submit just 1 or 2 claims and are not required to have 3 claims.
The table on Tab E will be filled in automatically using the information entered in the 'Productivity details' tab.
If an applicant is successful, the Rural Payments Agency will provide further guidance regarding data monitoring and reporting with the grant funding agreement.
A variable monetary penalty is a proportionate monetary penalty the Environment Agency may impose for the more serious cases of non-compliance.
The Environment Agency will always try to recover money spent on work connected with an offence and imposing a variable monetary penalty.
The Environment Agency may serve an enforcement cost recovery notice requiring the offender to pay the actual cost to the Environment Agency.

It is an offence to knowingly cause or permit any liquid or solid matter that is poisonous or injurious to fish, spawn, spawning areas or food of fish to enter waters containing fish, under Section 4(1).

A variable monetary penalty of £625 was imposed on 3 June 2025 in relation to a slurry pollution and fish kill incident on the Holebrook tributary of the River Okement at Winkleigh, Exeter, Devon on 25 January 2023.
The offender subject to the £625 variable monetary penalty imposed on 3 June 2025 also paid Environment Agency costs of £2,564.05.

A variable monetary penalty of £1,250 was imposed on 3 June 2025 in relation to a slurry pollution and fish kill incident on Coxbridge Brook in Glastonbury, Somerset between 30 March 2021 and 2 April 2021.
The offender subject to the £1,250 variable monetary penalty imposed on 3 June 2025 also paid Environment Agency costs of £4,078.54.
A fixed monetary penalty is a monetary penalty the Environment Agency may impose on a business or individual for cases of non-compliance.
The fixed monetary penalty is £300 for businesses.
The fixed monetary penalty is £100 for individuals.
Paying 50% of the fixed monetary penalty sum due within 28 days of receiving the notice of intent will clear legal responsibility for the fixed monetary penalty.

If representations have been made within the 28-day period but a final notice is served, legal responsibility for the fixed monetary penalty can still be cleared by paying 50% of the sum due within 28 days of the final notice.

Richard Barton, Anthony Barton and June Barton discharged liability for a fixed monetary penalty relating to failure to comply with Regulations 11(1) and 9 (storage of organic manure in a field at Brook Farm, Uffculme, Devon on or before 25 February 2025) by paying £150.

G A & S M Wainwright and Sons discharged liability for a fixed monetary penalty relating to failure to comply with Regulations 11(1) and 3 (spreading of manure on agricultural land at Upper House Farm and Ollerbrook Farm, Derbyshire on 10 January 2025) by paying £50.
The Environment Agency may issue a compliance notice on a person requiring them to take action to come back into compliance.

A compliance notice was imposed on 7 October 2019 for holding polychlorinated biphenyls on the Tube network in contravention of Regulation 4(1)(b)(i) of the Environmental Protection (Disposal of Polychlorinated Biphenyls and other Dangerous Substances) (England and Wales) Regulations 2000 after 31 December 2000.
A non-compliance penalty notice of £150,000 was served on 10 October 2024 for failing to comply with all the steps specified in the compliance notice (imposed 7 October 2019) by 31 December 2023.
The non-compliance penalty of £150,000 was paid on 7 November 2024 and the compliance notice was deemed to be completed on 13 December 2024.
The company subject to the compliance notice also paid Environment Agency costs of £29,690.
A person must notify the Environment Agency at least 14 days before constructing new storage for silage, slurry, or agricultural fuel oil.
A person must notify the Environment Agency at least 14 days before making substantial changes to an existing silage, slurry, or agricultural fuel oil store.
The store must not be within 10 metres of coastal waters or inland freshwaters.
The store must not be within 50 metres of a spring, well, or bore-hole.
A person notifying the Environment Agency may choose to provide additional information such as site plans, design drawings, manufacturer's specifications and guarantees, or soil tests.
If a person provides additional information, the Environment Agency may give bespoke guidance and recommendations to ensure the right store is built in the right place.
A person may also notify the Environment Agency by calling or writing to the local office.
The maximum quantity of waste received each day shall not exceed the designed storage capacity of the site.
Except for the auxiliary flare, there will be no combustion of biogas on site.
These rules do not allow any point source emission into land, surface waters or groundwater under control of the permit other than rain water.

The site must be at least 150 metres from a European site (within the meaning of Regulation 8 of the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017), a Site of Special Scientific Interest (including candidate or proposed sites), or a marine conservation zone.

The site must not be within a groundwater source protection zone 1 or 2, or within 50 metres of any well, spring or borehole used for the supply of water for human consumption (including private water supplies) where a source protection zone has not been defined.
The site must not be within 10 metres of any watercourse.
The site must not be within 50 metres of a Local Nature Reserve, Local Wildlife Site, Ancient Woodland or Scheduled Monument.

The site must not be within 50 metres of a site that has species or habitats of principal importance (as listed in Section 41 of the Natural Environment and Rural Communities Act 2006) that the Environment Agency considers at risk from this activity.
The site must not be located within a specified Air Quality Management Area.
Lagoons must be covered with a fixed cover and an appropriately designed air abatement and gas capture system to minimise the risk of ammonia and methane loss.

Activities shall be managed and operated in accordance with a management system, including the inspection and maintenance of equipment and engines, and point source emissions to air with emission limits requirement.
The activities shall not be carried out within a specified Air Quality Management Area for NOx.
Leak detection and repair programmes must be in place to mitigate and prevent methane emissions.
Gas pressure must be monitored to minimise the release of biogas, and all pressure relief systems must be inspected and calibrated as required by the permit.
Air abatement must be deployed to mitigate channelled bioaerosol emissions.
An odour management plan is required.
Fugitive emissions from slurry and digestate storage must be minimised by covering, and other fugitive emissions are not permitted.
All channelled emissions must be treated before release.
Fugitive source emissions of biogas shall be minimised by implementing a leak detection and repair programme.
All abatement systems must be designed, monitored and maintained to treat specified emissions and off-gases.
Noise and vibration shall be minimised and must not cause nuisance.
Flares must be kept to a minimum to reduce noise impact, and a record of use must be kept and reported.
Activities shall be managed and operated in accordance with a management system which includes site security measures to prevent unauthorised access.
All sites must have a DSEAR assessment which covers unauthorised access to the site.
Maintenance workers or contractors are not permitted on site without a suitable qualification and must have permission to do the work.
An accident management plan is required as part of the management system, covering fire and spillages.
A Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) or similar risk identification technique must be carried out to produce a schedule of planned maintenance, and must be documented.
Warning signs must be clearly displayed and personnel must know gas alarm procedures.
Visitors shall be accompanied on site.
Repair and maintenance may only take place if the plant manager gives permission and signs to that effect.
Fire control processes and procedures as set out in the DSEAR plan shall be communicated to the local fire service.
Leak detection schemes must be in place to prevent and minimise fugitive biogas release.
The site shall be secure.
Any breaches of site security shall be reported.
Contingency measures must be in place in the event of loss of plant.
The management system must include the risk management measures specified in the HAZOP and DSEAR plans and cover planned maintenance.
The measures and procedures for any potential accidents must be fully documented in the accident prevention and management plan, as part of the management system.
Training and regular 'toolbox' talks must be given to site operators so that all staff understand their role in an emergency.
The management system must include measures for bunding of tanks.
Gas pressure must be monitored continuously.
Storage of biogas and biomethane is restricted to limits as set out in the Control of Major Accident Hazard Regulations 2015 (COMAH).
Lightning conduction equipment must be put in place where necessary or required for insurance purposes.
Safe storage of activated charcoal and other combustible materials is required.
An impermeable surface is required for storage of all wastes.
No point source emissions are allowed to water.
Primary infrastructure used to contain oils or other chemicals must be bunded in line with CIRIA 736 and industry standards.
All storage areas shall be constructed to an approved standard.
Secondary containment must be in place and validated by a chartered engineer to CIRIA 736 standard.
All lagoons must be constructed to CIRIA 759 F standard.
Lagoons must be inspected to ensure appropriate freeboard is maintained.
All transfer of digestate and material must be supervised.

Activities cannot take place within groundwater source protection zone 1 or 2, or, if a source protection zone has not been defined, within 50 metres of any well, spring or borehole used for the supply of water for human consumption (including private water supplies).
All primary tanks must undergo a delegated inspection regime and process parameters must be monitored and understood by site operatives.
Lagoons must meet the silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil regulations or be CIRIA compliant.
Lagoons and tanks must be fitted with a high level alarm.
A 750 mm free board must be maintained in lagoons and tanks.
Storage tanks and lagoons must be covered and, where necessary, emissions must be abated.
Venting must be minimised by correctly fitting under and over pressure relief valves, and venting events must be recorded as abnormal events.
No fugitive releases of biogas or volatile organic compounds are permitted.
Operators must install a surplus gas burner or flare to combust surplus biogas.
Emissions of substances not controlled by emission limits (excluding odour and noise) shall not cause pollution.
A high risk rating requires additional assessment and active management.
A medium risk rating requires additional assessment and may need active management and/or monitoring.
Low and very low risk ratings will require a periodic review.
Land managers who use and discharge water must follow the legal requirements to avoid causing water pollution.

If you want to abstract more than 20 cubic metres of water per day from a surface source (river, stream or canal) or underground source (groundwater), you need an abstraction licence from the Environment Agency.
A land manager should regularly review their water needs and apply to vary their abstraction licence well in advance if water demand is likely to change.
If a licence holder wants to take advantage of high river flows in summer to top up a reservoir and their licence does not allow this, they must apply to vary their licence.
In an emergency situation such as a severe drought, the Environment Agency can change an abstraction licence if it authorises spray irrigation (a 'section 57 restriction').
Section 57 restrictions can stop or reduce all abstraction licences for spray irrigation within a water catchment.
Where a licence allows both spray irrigation and another purpose (such as vegetable washing), any section 57 restriction applies only to the spray irrigation abstraction.
Section 57 restrictions do not apply to trickle irrigators.
Section 57 restrictions do not apply to irrigation using water collected in winter storage reservoirs.
Section 57 restrictions do not apply to water used to supply pot grown plants which are unable to take moisture from the soil.
Section 57 restrictions do not apply to irrigation of covered crops (in glasshouses or poly tunnels).
An irrigator may be eligible for a charge reduction of up to 50% because of the uncertainty of potential section 57 restrictions, known as a 2 part tariff.
If a land manager discharges treated domestic sewage to surface water or to ground, they will need a permit from the Environment Agency unless they qualify for an exemption.

If a person cuts or uproots a substantial amount of vegetation in any inland freshwater (or so near that it falls in) and does not take reasonable steps to remove it from the water, they are carrying out a water discharge activity.
If a land manager plans to use herbicides to control aquatic or bank side weeds in England, they will need to get an agreement in advance from the Environment Agency.
A land manager must make sure that the herbicides they use will not affect anyone else using the site, or any water body or watercourse downstream of the site.
A land manager must make sure that herbicides will not affect any nature conservation areas.

To use a professional plant protection product in or near water, a person must have a recognised specified certificate (previously known as a certificate of competence) or be working under the direct supervision, for the purposes of training, of someone who has such a certificate.

A person does not need to hold a recognised specified certificate to use a professional plant protection product in or near water if they are working under the direct supervision of someone who holds one.
When using herbicides, a land manager must take precautions to protect the health of people, animals, wildlife, plants and the environment.
When using herbicides, a land manager must take precautions to avoid polluting water.
When using herbicides, a land manager must follow the instructions on the product label or in the published approval for the pesticide.
When using herbicides, a land manager must be trained in using herbicides in a safe, efficient and humane way and hold the appropriate certificate.
When using herbicides, a land manager must follow the code of practice for using plant protection products.
A land manager must apply for an aerial application permit if they want to apply pesticides from an aircraft.
When using pesticides, a land manager must follow the codes of practice available from the Chemicals Regulation Division.
If a person owns land above or with a watercourse running through it, they are a 'riparian owner' and have legal responsibilities.
A riparian owner may need permission to undertake maintenance work on watercourses.
If a land manager has a designated flood defence on their land, the Environment Agency may need access to carry out periodic maintenance.
A land manager has a legal responsibility to manage water use and avoid pollution.
A land manager must report pollution incidents to the Environment Agency immediately so that appropriate environmental protection measures can be put in place.
It is an offence to pollute surface waters or groundwater with waste water or polluted run-off from fields, including drain flow.
A land manager must make sure all slurry, including dirty water, is properly contained in a well-designed and constructed store.
Dirty yards must not drain directly into surface waters or groundwater.
A land manager must modify drains to separate clean and dirty water.
A land manager must not allow dirty yard runoff to enter soakaways, blind ditches and watercourses.
A land manager must use bunded areas or sealed drainage in any areas used for risky activities, such as refuelling, handling and mixing of pesticides, or washing machinery.
To treat non-hazardous pesticide washings in a biobed or biofilter, a land manager will need to register an exemption with the Environment Agency.
A land manager must have a permit from the Environment Agency if they want to dispose of pesticide washings to land.
A person who uses sheep dip should have a certificate of competence if they want to buy sheep dip and carry out or supervise dipping.
A person who uses sheep dip should only use products authorised by the Veterinary Medicines Directorate and follow the manufacturer's instructions.
A person who uses sheep dip should buy only enough sheep dip concentrate to meet their immediate needs.
A person who uses sheep dip should store unused sheep dip concentrate in a secure location.
A person who uses sheep dip should inspect sheep dip baths and draining areas regularly to make sure sheep dip cannot escape and any drips and splashes run back into the dip bath.
A sheep dip user should site their sheep dipping facilities at least 10 metres from a watercourse and more than 50 metres from a well, spring or borehole.
A sheep dip user should keep freshly dipped sheep in a holding field until they are dry.
A sheep dip user should rinse empty sheep dip containers at least 3 times and crush them so they cannot be used again.
A sheep dip user should dispose of waste sheep dip legally and as soon as possible after dipping.
A sheep dip user must have a permit from the Environment Agency if they want to dispose of waste sheep dip by spreading it on land.

If a person wants to treat waste organophosphate sheep dip with an approved organophosphate degrading enzyme, they will need to register an exemption with the Environment Agency and operate within the conditions set out in the exemption.
A land manager must comply with the storage of silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil (SSAFO) regulations to prevent water pollution.
SSAFO storage facilities should be sited at least 10 metres from inland freshwater or coastal water and have a 20 year life expectancy.

A land manager must notify the Environment Agency in writing about plans for any new, substantially enlarged or substantially reconstructed silage, slurry or agricultural fuel oil storage system at least 14 days before any construction begins.
If a land manager does not comply with a permit or exemption, or causes or knowingly allows water pollution, the Environment Agency will take enforcement action.
The Environment Agency may use criminal sanctions or civil sanctions against land managers who do not comply with permits or exemptions or who cause or knowingly allow water pollution.
